14. 14. 📝 Exam Answer Modes

1. Long Question (10 marks): "Discuss drug-induced SIADH — causes, diagnosis, and management."

  1. Definition & Criteria (2): Bartter & Schwartz criteria (6 points)
  2. Causes (2): Drug table (SSRIs, SNRIs, TCAs, Carbamazepine, Oxcarbazepine, Chemo, Antipsychotics, Opioids, Trimethoprim)
  3. Differential (1): CSW (hypovolaemic vs euvolaemic)
  4. Management (4): Severity-based (Severe: Hypertonic saline 3%, target ↑4–6/2h, max 8–10/24h; Moderate: Fluid restriction, demeclocycline/tolvaptan; Mild: Fluid restriction); STOP culprit drug; switch to low-risk alternatives (mirtazapine, bupropion, levetiracetam)
  5. Monitoring (1): Na⁺ frequency by severity

2. Short Question (5 marks): "Primary vs Secondary Adrenal Insufficiency — differentiation"

FeaturePrimary AISecondary AI
CortisolLowLow
ACTHHighLow/Normal
HyponatraemiaYesYes
PotassiumHighNormal
ReninHighNormal
Synacthen TestNo responseBlunted/delayed response
CausesAdrenal destruction (autoimmune, ketoconazole, mitotane)Pituitary/HPA (steroids, opioids, ICI hypophysitis)
FludrocortisoneRequiredNot required

3. Viva (2 min): "Patient on amiodarone 200mg daily for 1 year. TSH 0.01, fT4 35, fT3 12. Radioiodine uptake LOW. IL-6 elevated. Diagnosis? Management?"

  • AIT Type 2 (Destructive thyroiditis) — uptake LOW, IL-6 ↑
  • Prednisolone 30–40mg/day, taper over 2–3 months
  • Thionamides INEFFECTIVE (destructive, not hyperfunction)
  • Consider amiodarone cessation if feasible
  • Beta-blockers for symptomatic control
  • Monitor thyroid function monthly

4. Ward Round (30 sec): "Elderly on sertraline 100mg, Na⁺ 122, drowsy. Management?"

  • SSRI-induced SIADH (Moderate-Severe)
  • STOP sertraline
  • Fluid restrict 800–1000mL/day
  • Consider hypertonic saline if symptomatic (drowsy = symptomatic)
  • Switch to mirtazapine or bupropion (lower SIADH risk)
  • Monitor Na⁺ q6–12h

5. Last-Night Revision (1-liners):

  • SIADH = Euvolaemic hyponatraemia + High urine osmo/Na⁺; SSRIs, Carbamazepine, Chemo
  • Severe SIADH = Hypertonic saline 3%, ↑ Na⁺ 4–6/2h, max 8–10/24h
  • Steroid hyperglycaemia = Post-prandial pattern → Basal-bolus insulin (sliding scale inadequate)
  • Primary AI = Hyponatraemia + Hyperkalaemia + High ACTH → Fludrocortisone needed
  • Secondary AI = Hyponatraemia + Normal K⁺ + Low ACTH → No fludrocortisone
  • Adrenal crisis = Hydrocortisone 100mg IV + Saline 1L
  • AIT Type 1 = Iodine-induced, uptake HIGH → Thionamides + Perchlorate
  • AIT Type 2 = Destructive thyroiditis, uptake LOW → Prednisolone (Thionamides NO)
  • Amiodarone hypo = Levothyroxine, continue amiodarone
  • GIOP = Pred ≥7.5mg ×3mo → Ca/VitD + Bisphosphonate
  • ONJ = IV Bisphosphonates/Denosumab > oral; exposed bone >8wks
  • Hyperprolactinaemia = Risperidone > Haloperidol → Switch to Aripiprazole/Quetiapine

15. 15. 📚 Summary Card

ENDOCRINE EMERGENCY TRIAD:
1. SIADH (Severe) → Hypertonic saline 3%, ↑ Na⁺ 4–6/2h, STOP culprit
2. ADRENAL CRISIS → Hydrocortisone 100mg IV + Saline 1L + Fludrocortisone (primary)
3. AIT TYPE 2 → Uptake LOW → Prednisolone (NOT thionamides)
STEROID HYPERGLYCAEMIA = Basal-bolus insulin (sliding scale NO)
PRIMARY AI = HyperK + High ACTH + Fludrocortisone
SECONDARY AI = Normal K + Low ACTH + No Fludrocortisone
GIOP = Pred ≥7.5mg ×3mo → Bisphosphonate
HYPERPROLACTINAEMIA = Risperidone → Switch to Aripiprazole/Quetiapine

16. 16. ❓ MCQs (15)

  1. SIADH diagnostic criteria — which is NOT required?

A. Hyponatraemia

B. Low serum osmolality

C. High urine osmolality

D. Hypovolaemia ✓

E. Normal thyroid/adrenal function

  1. Drug class MOST commonly causing SIADH in elderly:

A. Antihypertensives

B. Antidepressants (SSRIs/SNRIs/TCAs) ✓

C. Statins

D. PPIs

E. Bisphosphonates

  1. Severe SIADH (Na⁺ 115, seizures) — fluid of choice:

A. 0.9% NaCl

B. 3% Hypertonic saline ✓

C. 5% Dextrose

D. 0.45% NaCl

E. Hartmann's

  1. Maximum safe rate of Na⁺ correction in 24 hours:

A. 4–6 mmol/L

B. 8–10 mmol/L ✓

C. 12–15 mmol/L

D. 15–20 mmol/L

E. No limit

  1. Steroid-induced hyperglycaemia — insulin regimen of choice for high-dose steroids:

A. Sliding scale

B. Basal-bolus ✓

C. Once-daily glargine only

C. Metformin alone

E. Gliclazide only

  1. Primary adrenal insufficiency — characteristic electrolytes:

A. Hyponatraemia, Hypokalaemia

B. Hyponatraemia, Hyperkalaemia ✓

C. Hypernatraemia, Hyperkalaemia

D. Normal Na⁺, High K⁺

E. Low Na⁺, Low K⁺

  1. Acute adrenal crisis — immediate hydrocortisone dose:

A. 25mg IV

B. 50mg IV

C. 100mg IV ✓

D. 200mg IV

E. 500mg IV

  1. AIT Type 1 vs Type 2 — radioiodine uptake:

A. Both high

B. Both low

C. Type 1 high, Type 2 low ✓

D. Type 1 low, Type 2 high

E. Neither affected

  1. AIT Type 2 — first-line treatment:

A. Carbimazole

B. Prednisolone 30–40mg/day ✓

C. Propylthiouracil

D. Beta-blockers only

E. Amiodarone cessation only

  1. Amiodarone-induced hypothyroidism — management:

A. Stop amiodarone

B. Levothyroxine replacement, continue amiodarone ✓

C. Prednisolone

D. Carbimazole

E. Radioiodine

  1. Glucocorticoid-induced osteoporosis — treatment threshold:

A. Prednisolone ≥5mg/day ×1 month

B. Prednisolone ≥7.5mg/day ×3 months ✓

C. Prednisolone ≥10mg/day ×6 months

D. Any dose ×1 year

E. Only if DEXA T-score <-2.5

  1. ONJ risk — highest with:

A. Oral alendronate

B. IV zoledronate ✓

C. Oral risedronate

D. Calcium/vitamin D

E. Denosumab (equal to oral)

  1. Hyperprolactinaemia — antipsychotic with HIGHEST risk:

A. Quetiapine

B. Aripiprazole

C. Clozapine

D. Risperidone ✓

E. Olanzapine

  1. Lithium-induced thyroid dysfunction — most common:

A. Thyrotoxicosis

B. Hypothyroidism ✓

C. Thyroiditis

D. Thyroid cancer

E. Euthyroid goitre only

  1. Checkpoint inhibitor thyroiditis — typical pattern:

A. Permanent hyperthyroidism

B. Transient thyrotoxicosis → permanent hypothyroidism ✓

C. Permanent hyperthyroidism

D. Euthyroid sick syndrome

E. Thyroid storm

17. 17. 🃏 Flashcards (Anki-ready)

FrontBack
SIADH criteriaHyponatraemia, low serum osmo, high urine osmo, high urine Na, euvolaemic, normal thyroid/adrenal/renal
SIADH top drugsSSRIs/SNRIs/TCAs, Carbamazepine/Oxcarbazepine, Cyclophosphamide/Vincristine, Antipsychotics, Opioids
Severe SIADH RxHypertonic saline 3%, ↑ Na 4–6/2h, max 8–10/24h, furosemide, ICU
Steroid hyperglycaemiaPost-prandial pattern → Basal-bolus insulin
Primary AI electrolytesHyponatraemia + Hyperkalaemia
Secondary AI electrolytesHyponatraemia + Normal K⁺
Adrenal crisisHydrocortisone 100mg IV + Saline 1L + Fludrocortisone (primary)
AIT Type 1Iodine-induced, uptake HIGH → Thionamides + Perchlorate
AIT Type 2Destructive thyroiditis, uptake LOW → Prednisolone (thionamides NO)
Amiodarone hypoLevothyroxine, continue amiodarone
Lithium thyroidHypothyroidism (common)
ICI thyroiditisThyrotoxic → permanent hypothyroidism
GIOP thresholdPred ≥7.5mg/day ×3mo → Ca/VitD + Bisphosphonate
ONJ riskIV Bisphosphonates > oral; Denosumab; Bevacizumab
HyperprolactinaemiaRisperidone highest → Switch to Aripiprazole/Quetiapine

18. 18. ✅ Answer Keys

1. MCQs

  1. D — SIADH is euvolaemic (not hypovolaemic)
  2. B — Antidepressants most common in elderly
  3. B — 3% Hypertonic saline
  4. B — Max 8–10 mmol/L/24h
  5. B — Basal-bolus for high-dose steroids
  6. B — Primary AI = Hyperkalaemia
  7. C — 100mg IV hydrocortisone
  8. C — Type 1 uptake high, Type 2 uptake low
  9. B — Prednisolone for Type 2
  10. B — Levothyroxine, continue amiodarone
  11. B — ≥7.5mg ×3mo
  12. B — IV zoledronate highest ONJ risk
  13. D — Risperidone highest prolactin
  14. B — Lithium = hypothyroidism
  15. B — Thyrotoxic → permanent hypothyroid

File: /mnt/tb/Medicine/Clinical Therapeutics and Good Prescribing/ADRs/Endocrine ADRs.md | Status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 2.1.09 Haematological Adrs

Status: full-fcps-mrcp-note | Chapter: 2 — Clinical Therapeutics and Good Prescribing | Heading: Adverse Drug Reactions → System-Specific Patterns | Exam Priority: ⭐⭐⭐ HIGH (Agranulocytosis, Thrombocytopenia, HIT, Haemolysis — VIVA, emergency recognition)

1. 1. 🎯 Learning Objectives

  • [ ] Classify drug-induced haematological toxicity by cell line
  • [ ] Differentiate immune vs non-immune thrombocytopenia (HIT, DITP, TTP)
  • [ ] Recognise agranulocytosis — immediate management
  • [ ] Identify drug-induced haemolysis (G6PD, immune, microangiopathic)
  • [ ] Apply monitoring protocols (clozapine, carbamazepine, antithyroid drugs)
  • [ ] Execute emergency algorithms (HIT 4Ts, agranulocytosis)

2. 2. 📊 Classification by Cell Line

Cell LineReactionMechanismKey Drugs
NeutrophilsAgranulocytosis (<0.5×10⁹/L)Immune (hapten/antibody) / Direct toxicityClozapine, Carbamazepine, Sulfonamides, Antithyroid (carbimazole, PTU), Ticlopidine, Chemotherapy, Valproate
Neutropenia (0.5–1.5×10⁹/L)Dose-dependent / IdiosyncraticChemotherapy, G-CSF, Antibiotics, Antivirals
PlateletsThrombocytopenia (<100×10⁹/L)Immune (DITP), HIT, TMA, Bone marrow suppressionHeparin (HIT), Quinine, GPIIb/IIIa inhibitors, Valproate, Carbamazepine, Sulfonamides, Gold, Heparin, Chemotherapy
Red CellsHaemolytic AnaemiaImmune (Type II), G6PD deficiency (oxidative), Microangiopathic (TMA)Penicillins (AIHA), Cephalosporins, Methyldopa, NSAIDs, Quinidine, Sulfonamides (G6PD), Dapsone, Methylene blue, Clopidogrel (TMA)
Pure Red Cell Aplasia (PRCA)Immune / T-cell mediatedErythropoietin (anti-EPO antibodies), Azathioprine, Phenytoin, Carbamazepine, Isoniazid
All LinesAplastic AnaemiaIdiosyncratic / Stem cell toxicityChloramphenicol, Carbamazepine, Phenytoin, Gold, Benzene, Chemotherapy

3. 3. ⚠️ Agranulocytosis — Medical Emergency

FeaturesAgranulocytosis (<0.5 × 10⁹/L)Neutropenia (0.5–1.5 × 10⁹/L)
RiskLife-threatening sepsisInfection risk ↑
OnsetDays–weeks (immune); immediate (chemo)Variable
PresentationFever, sore throat, sepsis, mouth ulcersOften asymptomatic
Immediate ActionSTOP drug, IV antibiotics, G-CSF, reverse isolation, blood culturesStop drug if severe; monitor; G-CSF if <1.0

1. High-Risk Drugs for Agranulocytosis

DrugMonitoringRisk
ClozapineMandatory: Weekly ×18wks → fortnightly ×1yr → monthly~1% risk; register; restart only if ANC>2.0
CarbamazepineBaseline + periodic (no formal mandate)~0.1%; HLA-B*15:02 screening (Asians)
Antithyroid (Carbimazole/PTU)Patient education (sore throat → urgent FBC)~0.1–0.5%; no routine monitoring
SulfonamidesClinical vigilanceIdiosyncratic
TiclopidineFBC 2-weekly ×3mo~1%; largely replaced by clopidogrel
ChemotherapyProtocol-driven (nadir day 7–14)Expected; G-CSF support

2. Management Algorithm

⬇ Clinical Pathway
Patient on high-risk drug presents with FEVER
↓
**URGENT FBC + DIFFERENTIAL**
↓
ANC <0.5 × 10⁹/L?
↓
**AGRANULOCYTOSIS** **STOP DRUG IMMEDIATELY** **IV Broad-spectrum antibiotics** (Piperacillin-tazobactam / Meropenem) **G-CSF** (Filgrastim 5mcg/kg/day until ANC>1.0) **Reverse isolation** Blood/urine cultures No routine antifungals unless persistent fever>4-5d
**SEVERE NEUTROPENIA** Stop drug IV antibiotics if febrile G-CSF consideration Close monitoring
**MODERATE NEUTROPENIA** Stop drug if possible Oral antibiotics if febrile Monitor FBC 2-3x/week
**NOT NEUTROPENIC** Evaluate other causes of fever Continue drug if benefit>risk

4. 4. 🩸 Drug-Induced Thrombocytopenia (DITP) & HIT

1. DITP (Non-HIT Immune Thrombocytopenia)

FeatureDITP
MechanismDrug-dependent antibody (IgG) binds platelet GPIIb/IIIa or Ib/IX → FcγR-mediated clearance
Onset5–14 days (first exposure); hours (re-exposure)
Platelet CountOften <20×10⁹/L (severe)
Key DrugsQuinine/Quinidine, Sulfonamides, GPIIb/IIIa inhibitors (Abciximab, Eptifibatide, Tirofiban), Valproate, Carbamazepine, Heparin (non-HIT), Gold, Rifampicin
DiagnosisDrug cessation → platelet recovery within 3–7 days; drug-dependent antibody assay (specialised)
ManagementSTOP drug → platelets recover in days; IVIG if bleeding/severe; avoid re-exposure

2. Heparin-Induced Thrombocytopenia (HIT) — Critical

FeatureHIT (Type II)
MechanismIgG antibody to PF4-heparin complex → platelet activation → prothrombotic state (NOT bleeding)
Onset5–14 days (UFH > LMWH > Fondaparinux)
Platelet Count↓ 30–50% from baseline (rarely <20×10⁹/L); thrombosis > bleeding
4Ts Score (Pre-test Probability)
Thrombocytopenia>50% fall & nadir ≥20 = 2; 30–50% or nadir 10–19 = 1; <30% or nadir <10 = 0
Timing5–10d = 2; ≤1d (prior exposure) = 1; >10d = 0
ThrombosisNew thrombosis = 2; progressive/recurrent = 1; none = 0
Other causesNone = 2; possible = 1; definite = 0
Score4Ts ≥4 = High; 3–4 = Intermediate; ≤2 = Low

3. HIT Management Algorithm

⬇ Clinical Pathway
Suspect HIT: Thrombocytopenia 5-14d post-heparin
↓
Calculate 4Ts Score
↓
4Ts Score
↓
& E & F
**STOP ALL HEPARIN** (UFH, LMWH, flushes) **Start non-heparin anticoagulant**: Argatroban (IV, hepatic adj) / Bivalirudin (IV, renal adj) / Fondaparinux (SC, off-label) Send **HIT antibody (PF4 ELISA)** If ELISA +ve → continue anticoagulation
**STOP ALL HEPARIN IMMEDIATELY** **Start non-heparin anticoagulant** (Argatroban/Bivalirudin/Fondaparinux) Send HIT antibody **Do NOT wait for result** **No platelet transfusion** (unless active bleed) **No warfarin alone** (risk WISN — wait for platelet recovery >150)
↓
**Anticoagulation duration**: 3 months if thrombosis; 4–6 weeks if isolated HIT Transition to DOAC/Warfarin after platelet recovery >150

4. HIT vs DITP Comparison

FeatureHITDITP
MechanismPF4-heparin IgG → platelet activationDrug-dependent IgG → platelet clearance
ClinicalThrombosis >> BleedingBleeding >> Thrombosis
Platelet Count↓30–50%, rarely <20Often <20
Timing5–14 days5–14 days (first); hours (re-exposure)
AnticoagulationMust continue (non-heparin)Stop drug; no anticoagulation needed
Platelet TransfusionContraindicated (unless life-threatening bleed)OK if bleeding
WarfarinDelay until platelets >150 (WISN risk)N/A

5. 5. 🔴 Haemolytic Anaemia

1. Immune Haemolytic Anaemia (Type II)

TypeMechanismDrugsFeatures
Penicillin-typeDrug binds RBC membrane → antibody to drug-RBC complexPenicillins, CephalosporinsDAT IgG + C3d (drug-dependent); spherocytes; ↓ drug = recovery
Methyldopa-typeAutoantibody to RBC (Rh-like) — true autoimmuneMethyldopaDAT IgG + C3d (no drug needed); warm AIHA; may persist months after stop
Ternary ComplexDrug-antibody complex binds RBC weaklyCephalosporins, RifampicinDAT C3d only (IgG weak); high-titre antibody

2. G6PD Deficiency (Oxidative Haemolysis)

DrugRisk
DapsoneHigh
Sulfonamides (TMP-SMX, sulfonylureas)High
Primaquine / TafenoquineContraindicated
Methylene blueHigh
RasburicaseContraindicated
High-dose Vitamin C / NitrofurantoinModerate

3. Microangiopathic Haemolytic Anaemia (MAHA/TMA)

DrugContext
Ciclosporin / Tacrolimus / SirolimusTransplant (dose-dependent)
Clopidogrel / TiclopidineTTP-like (ADAMTS13 inhibition)
QuinineImmune-mediated
Gemcitabine / VEGF inhibitorsChemotherapy
HITPF4-heparin → thrombosis + MAHA

6. 6. 🎯 FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

EmergencyKey DrugImmediate Action
Agranulocytosis + FeverClozapine, Carbimazole, CarbamazepineSTOP drug, IV antibiotics, G-CSF, reverse isolation
HIT (4Ts ≥4) + ThrombosisUFH, LMWHSTOP heparin, START argatroban/bivalirudin/fondaparinux, NO warfarin alone, NO platelet transfusion
DITP + Bleeding (Platelets <10)Quinine, GPIIb/IIIa, ValproateSTOP drug, IVIG 1g/kg ×2d, platelets if life-threatening bleed
G6PD HaemolysisDapsone, Sulfonamides, PrimaquineSTOP drug, transfuse if severe, folic acid, avoid oxidants
Methyldopa AIHAMethyldopaSTOP drug, steroids if severe, may take months to resolve

7. 7. ❓ Viva Questions (12)

QAnswer
1. Define agranulocytosis. Immediate management of febrile patient on clozapine?ANC <0.5×10⁹/L; STOP clozapine, IV broad-spectrum antibiotics, G-CSF 5mcg/kg/day, reverse isolation, cultures
2. Clozapine monitoring schedule?Weekly ×18wks → fortnightly ×1yr → monthly thereafter; ANC must be >1.5 (green), 1.0–1.5 (amber), <1.0 (red — stop)
3. HIT vs DITP — key clinical difference?HIT = thrombotic (prothrombotic); DITP = bleeding (thrombocytopenia)
4. 4Ts score components? Threshold for high probability?Thrombocytopenia, Timing, Thrombosis, Other causes; ≥4 = High
5. HIT management — why NO warfarin alone?Warfarin-induced skin necrosis (WISN) — protein C depletion before factor II/X; must overlap with parenteral anticoagulant until platelets >150
6. HIT — why NO platelet transfusion?Fuels thrombosis (platelets = prothrombotic fuel) — only if life-threatening bleeding
7. Penicillin-type vs Methyldopa-type immune haemolysis?Penicillin: drug-dependent antibody (DAT IgG+C3d, recover on stop); Methyldopa: true autoantibody (DAT IgG+C3d, warm AIHA, persists after stop)
8. G6PD deficiency — 3 high-risk drugs contraindicated?Primaquine/tafenoquine, dapsone, rasburicase; also sulfonamides, methylene blue
9. TMA — drug causes? Management of tacrolimus TMA?CNIs (tacro/ciclo/siro), clopidogrel, quinine, gemcitabine, HIT; Tacro TMA: ↓ dose or stop, consider sirolimus/mTOR switch
10. DITP — platelet recovery time after drug cessation?3–7 days (if immune); recover quickly once antibody cleared
11. Carbimazole/PTU agranulocytosis — monitoring?No routine FBC; Patient education: sore throat/fever → urgent FBC same day
12. Valproate thrombocytopenia — mechanism? Dose relationship?Dose-dependent bone marrow suppression + platelet dysfunction; >100 mcg/mL or >50mg/kg/day ↑ risk; monitor FBC

8. 8. 🤯 Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
HIT = Thrombotic vs BleedingHIT = PROTHROMBOTIC (thrombosis >> bleeding) — "HIT = Hypercoagulable"
HIT vs DITP platelet countHIT: ↓30–50% (rarely <20); DITP: often <20
HIT antibody = PF4-heparinPF4-heparin complex → IgG → platelet activation via FcγRIIa
Methyldopa AIHA vs Penicillin AIHAMethyldopa = true autoantibody (persists); Penicillin = drug-dependent (recovers on stop)
G6PD drugsOxidative drugs: dapsone, sulfonamides, primaquine, methylene blue, rasburicase
Clozapine agranulocytosisMandatory monitoring register; cannot prescribe without

Mnemonics:

  • "4Ts" = Thrombocytopenia, Timing, Thrombosis, T (o)ther causes
  • "HIT = HYPERCOAGULABLE" = Heparin Induced Thrombosis (not bleeding)
  • "ARGATROBAN/BIVALIRUDIN/FONDAPARINUX" = HIT anticoagulants (NO heparin, NO warfarin alone)
  • "WISN" = Warfarin-Induced Skin Necrosis (start warfarin ONLY after platelets >150 in HIT)
  • "PLATELET RECOVERY 3-7 DAYS" = DITP platelet recovery after drug stop
  • "G6PD NO-GO" = Primaquine, Dapsone, Sulfonamides, Methylene blue, Rasburicase
  • "METHYLDOPA = TRUE AUTO" = Autoantibody (persists after stop)
  • "PENICILLIN = DRUG-DEPENDENT" = Antibody needs drug on RBC (recovers on stop)

9. 9. 🧠 Mind Map (Mermaid)

Haematological ADRs
Neutrophils
Agranulocytosis (<0.5)
– Clozapine (mandatory monitoring)
– Carbamazepine
– Antithyroid (carbimazole/PTU)
– Sulfonamides
– Ticlopidine
– Chemotherapy
Management: STOP, IV Abx, G-CSF, isolation
Platelets
DITP (immune)
– Quinine, GPIIb/IIIa, Valproate, Carbamazepine, Heparin (non-HIT)
– Recovery 3-7d post stop
– IVIG if bleeding
HIT (Type II)
– PF4-heparin IgG
– 5-14d, thrombosis > bleeding
– 4Ts score
– Stop heparin → Argatroban/Bivalirudin/Fondaparinux
– NO warfarin alone until platelets >150
– NO platelet transfusion
Red Cells
Immune Haemolysis (Type II)
– Penicillin-type: drug-dependent (recovers on stop)
– Methyldopa-type: true autoantibody (persists)
G6PD Deficiency (oxidative)
– Dapsone, Sulfonamides, Primaquine, Methylene blue, Rasburicase
MAHA/TMA
– CNIs (tacro/ciclo/siro), Clopidogrel, Quinine, Gemcitabine, HIT

10. 10. 📅 Spaced Repetition Tracker

ReviewDateScoreNext
11d
23d
31w
42w
51m
63m

11. 11. 🧪 Self-Test Scorecard

SectionMaxScore
Classification table8
Agranulocytosis + monitoring10
HIT (4Ts, management)12
DITP vs HIT8
Haemolytic anaemia types10
G6PD drugs6
Viva answers12
Total66

Target: ≥53/66 (80%)

12. 12. 📝 Exam Answer Modes

1. Long Question (10 marks): "Discuss heparin-induced thrombocytopenia (HIT) — pathophysiology, diagnosis, and management."

  1. Pathophysiology (2): PF4-heparin complex → IgG antibody → platelet activation via FcγRIIa → thrombin generation, prothrombotic state
  2. Diagnosis (3): 4Ts score (Thrombocytopenia, Timing, Thrombosis, Other causes); High ≥4, Intermediate 3–4, Low ≤2; PF4 ELISA confirmation
  3. Management (4): STOP all heparin immediately; start non-heparin anticoagulant (argatroban/bivalirudin/fondaparinux); NO warfarin alone until platelets >150 (WISN risk); NO platelet transfusion unless life-threatening bleed; anticoagulate 3mo if thrombosis, 4–6wk if isolated HIT
  4. HIT vs DITP (1): HIT = thrombotic; DITP = bleeding; DITP platelet recovery 3–7 days

2. Short Question (5 marks): "HIT 4Ts Score"

Component2 Points1 Point0 Points
Thrombocytopenia>50% fall, nadir ≥2030–50% fall or nadir 10–19<30% fall or nadir <10
Timing5–10 days≤1 day (prior exposure) or >10 days—
ThrombosisNew thrombosisProgressive/recurrentNone
Other causesNone apparentPossibleDefinite

≥4 = High probability; 3–4 = Intermediate; ≤2 = Low

3. Viva (2 min): "Patient on UFH 7 days, platelets 80 (baseline 250), new DVT. 4Ts? Management?"

  • 4Ts: Thrombocytopenia 2 (>50% fall, nadir ≥20), Timing 2 (5–10d), Thrombosis 2 (new DVT), Other causes 2 (none) = 8 = HIGH
  • STOP all heparin immediately
  • Start argatroban (IV, hepatic adjust) or bivalirudin (IV, renal adjust) or fondaparinux (SC)
  • Send PF4 ELISA
  • NO warfarin alone — wait for platelets >150
  • NO platelet transfusion
  • Anticoagulate 3 months (thrombosis present)

4. Ward Round (30 sec): "Patient on clozapine 3 months, temp 38.5°C. Immediate steps?"

  • Agranulocytosis until proven otherwise
  • URGENT FBC + differential
  • If ANC <0.5 → STOP clozapine, IV piperacillin-tazobactam/meropenem, G-CSF 5mcg/kg, reverse isolation, cultures

5. Last-Night Revision (1-liners):

  • Agranulocytosis = ANC<0.5 → STOP drug, IV Abx, G-CSF, isolation
  • Clozapine: Weekly×18wk → 2wk×1yr → monthly
  • Carbimazole: No routine FBC; sore throat = urgent FBC
  • HIT = PF4-heparin IgG → thrombotic (not bleeding)
  • 4Ts: Thrombocytopenia, Timing, Thrombosis, Other causes; ≥4 = High
  • HIT management: STOP heparin → Argatroban/Bivalirudin/Fondaparinux; NO warfarin alone; NO platelet transfusion
  • DITP = bleeding, platelets often <20, recovery 3–7d, IVIG if bleeding
  • Penicillin AIHA = drug-dependent (recovers); Methyldopa AIHA = true auto (persists)
  • G6PD: Dapsone, Sulfonamides, Primaquine, Methylene blue, Rasburicase
  • TMA: CNIs, Clopidogrel, Quinine, Gemcitabine, HIT

13. 13. 📚 Summary Card

HAEMATOLOGICAL EMERGENCY TRIAD:
1. AGRANULOCYTOSIS + FEVER → STOP drug, IV Abx, G-CSF, Isolation (Clozapine, Carbimazole, CZP)
2. HIT (4Ts ≥4) → STOP heparin, Argatroban/Bivalirudin, NO Warfarin alone, NO Platelets (Thrombosis not bleeding!)
3. DITP + BLEEDING → STOP drug, IVIG 1g/kg (Platelets often <20, recovery 3–7d)
G6PD: Avoid Primaquine, Dapsone, Sulfonamides, Methylene blue, Rasburicase
Methyldopa AIHA = True autoantibody (persists); Penicillin AIHA = Drug-dependent (recovers)

14. 14. ❓ MCQs (15)

  1. Agranulocytosis is defined as absolute neutrophil count (ANC):

A. <1.0 × 10⁹/L

B. <0.5 × 10⁹/L ✓

C. <1.5 × 10⁹/L

D. <0.2 × 10⁹/L

E. <0.1 × 10⁹/L

  1. Clozapine monitoring schedule after 1 year of treatment:

A. Weekly

B. Fortnightly

C. Monthly ✓

D. Every 3 months

E. No monitoring needed

  1. Carbimazole-induced agranulocytosis — recommended monitoring:

A. Weekly FBC

B. Fortnightly FBC

C. No routine FBC; patient education (sore throat → urgent FBC) ✓

D. Monthly FBC

E. Only if symptomatic

  1. HIT (Type II) — primary clinical manifestation:

A. Severe bleeding

B. Thrombosis (arterial and venous) ✓

C. Asymptomatic thrombocytopenia

D. DIC

E. Haemolysis

  1. 4Ts score — high probability threshold:

A. ≥3

B. ≥4 ✓

C. ≥5

D. ≥6

E. ≥2

  1. HIT management — which is CONTRAINDICATED?

A. Argatroban

B. Bivalirudin

C. Fondaparinux

D. Warfarin alone (without parenteral overlap) ✓

E. Platelet transfusion (if life-threatening bleed)

  1. HIT — platelet transfusion is:

A. Routinely recommended

B. Contraindicated (fuels thrombosis) ✓

C. Required if platelets <20

D. Required if platelets <10

D. Only if bleeding

  1. Penicillin-type vs Methyldopa-type immune haemolysis — key difference:

A. Penicillin causes intravascular haemolysis only

B. Methyldopa produces true autoantibody (persists after stop); Penicillin is drug-dependent (recovers on stop) ✓

C. Methyldopa causes Coombs-negative haemolysis

D. Penicillin causes warm AIHA only

E. No difference

  1. G6PD deficiency — drug CONTRAINDICATED:

A. Dapsone

B. Primaquine ✓

C. Sulfonamides

D. Methylene blue

E. Nitrofurantoin

  1. DITP — platelet recovery after drug cessation:

A. 24–48 hours

B. 3–7 days ✓

C. 2–3 weeks

D. 1–2 months

E. Never recovers

  1. Valproate thrombocytopenia — mechanism:

A. Immune-mediated platelet destruction

B. Dose-dependent bone marrow suppression + platelet dysfunction ✓

C. Heparin-induced

D. TMA

E. Dilutional

  1. Tacrolimus/ciclosporin — associated haematological toxicity:

A. Agranulocytosis

B. Thrombotic microangiopathy (TMA) ✓

C. Pure red cell aplasia

D. Immune haemolytic anaemia

E. Aplastic anaemia

  1. Clopidogrel-associated TMA — mechanism:

A. Dose-dependent endothelial injury

B. ADAMTS13 inhibition (immune-mediated) ✓

C. Complement activation

D. Drug-dependent antibody

E. Oxidative stress

  1. Drug-induced pure red cell aplasia (PRCA) — associated with:

A. Heparin

B. Erythropoietin (anti-EPO antibodies) ✓

C. Warfarin

D. Aspirin

E. Clopidogrel

  1. HIT antibody target:

A. Platelet GPIIb/IIIa

B. PF4-heparin complex ✓

C. von Willebrand factor

D. Fibrinogen

E. Protein C

15. 15. 🃏 Flashcards (Anki-ready)

FrontBack
Agranulocytosis definitionANC <0.5 × 10⁹/L
Clozapine monitoringWeekly×18wk → Fortnightly×1yr → Monthly
Carbimazole agranulocytosisNo routine FBC; sore throat = urgent FBC
Ticlopidine monitoringFBC 2-weekly ×3mo (largely replaced by clopidogrel)
HIT mechanismPF4-heparin complex → IgG → platelet activation (FcγRIIa) → prothrombotic
4Ts componentsThrombocytopenia, Timing, Thrombosis, Other causes
4Ts high probability≥4
HIT managementSTOP heparin → Argatroban/Bivalirudin/Fondaparinux; NO warfarin alone; NO platelets
WISNWarfarin-induced skin necrosis (protein C depletion)
DITPDrug-dependent IgG → platelet clearance; recovery 3–7d; bleeding > thrombosis
DITP key drugsQuinine, GPIIb/IIIa inhibitors, Valproate, Carbamazepine
Penicillin AIHADrug-dependent antibody → recovers on stop
Methyldopa AIHATrue autoantibody → warm AIHA, persists months
G6PD drugs to avoidPrimaquine, Dapsone, Sulfonamides, Methylene blue, Rasburicase
TMA drugsCNIs (tacro/ciclo/siro), Clopidogrel, Quinine, Gemcitabine, HIT
Valproate thrombocytopeniaDose-dependent BM suppression + platelet dysfunction
PRCA drugsEPO (anti-EPO Abs), Azathioprine, Phenytoin, Carbamazepine, INH

16. 16. ✅ Answer Keys

1. MCQs

  1. B — ANC <0.5 × 10⁹/L
  2. C — Monthly after 1 year
  3. C — No routine FBC; patient education
  4. B — HIT = thrombotic (prothrombotic)
  5. B — 4Ts ≥4 = High
  6. D — Warfarin alone contraindicated (WISN risk)
  7. B — Platelet transfusion contraindicated (fuels thrombosis)
  8. B — Methyldopa = true autoantibody (persists); Penicillin = drug-dependent (recovers)
  9. B — Primaquine contraindicated in G6PD
  10. B — DITP platelet recovery 3–7 days
  11. B — Valproate = dose-dependent BM suppression
  12. B — CNIs → TMA
  13. B — Clopidogrel → ADAMTS13 inhibition
  14. B — EPO → anti-EPO antibodies
  15. B — PF4-heparin complex

File: /mnt/tb/Medicine/Clinical Therapeutics and Good Prescribing/ADRs/Haematological ADRs.md | Status: full-fcps-mrcp-note

▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 2.1.10 Hepatic Adrs

Status: full-fcps-mrcp-note | Chapter: 2 — Clinical Therapeutics and Good Prescribing | Heading: Adverse Drug Reactions → System-Specific Patterns | Exam Priority: ⭐⭐⭐ HIGHEST (Hy's Law, RUCAM, pattern recognition, VIVA staple)

1. 1. 🎯 Learning Objectives

  • [ ] Classify DILI by biochemical pattern (Hepatocellular, Cholestatic, Mixed)
  • [ ] Apply Hy's Law for prognosis
  • [ ] Use RUCAM for causality assessment
  • [ ] Identify high-risk drugs for each pattern
  • [ ] Differentiate intrinsic vs idiosyncratic DILI
  • [ ] Execute monitoring and management algorithms

2. 2. 📊 DILI Classification by Biochemical Pattern

PatternDefinition (R-value)Typical DrugsPrognosis
HepatocellularR = ALT/ULN ÷ ALP/ULN ≥ 5Paracetamol (OD), Isoniazid, Valproate, Nevirapine, Flucloxacillin (some), Methotrexate, HalothaneWorse — higher mortality, transplant
CholestaticR ≤ 2Flucloxacillin, Amoxicillin-Clavulanate, Azathioprine, Chlorpromazine, Anabolic steroids, OCP, Sulfonamides, AllopurinolBetter — prolonged but lower mortality
Mixed2 < R < 5Diclofenac, Sulfonamides, Allopurinol, Phenytoin, Carbamazepine, Tuberculosis drugsIntermediate
R-value = (ALT ÷ ULN) ÷ (ALP ÷ ULN)

3. 3. ⚠️ Hy's Law — Prognostic Rule (Exam Essential)

CriteriaSignificance
1. Hepatocellular injury (ALT >3× ULN)
2. Jaundice (Total Bilirubin >2× ULN)
3. No obstruction (imaging negative)
4. No other cause (viral, autoimmune, etc.)

If ALL 4 met → 10–50% mortality or transplant

Hy's Law = "The law of mortality" — stop drug immediately, transplant evaluation

4. 4. 🧬 Intrinsic vs Idiosyncratic DILI

FeatureIntrinsic (Type A)Idiosyncratic (Type B)
Dose-dependenceYes — predictableNo — unpredictable
IncidenceCommonRare
LatencyShort (hours–days)Variable (weeks–months)
MechanismDirect toxicity / metaboliteImmune / metabolic idiosyncrasy
ExamplesParacetamol overdose, Methotrexate (high-dose), Niacin, Valproate (microvesicular steatosis)Isoniazid, Flucloxacillin, Augmentin, Diclofenac, Allopurinol, Carbamazepine, Nevirapine
RechallengeToxic at same doseAbsolutely contraindicated

5. 5. 💊 High-Risk Drugs by Pattern

1. Hepatocellular (R ≥ 5)

DrugKey Features
Paracetamol (overdose)NAPQI toxicity → centrilobular necrosis; NAC antidote; Rumack-Matthew nomogram
IsoniazidHepatocellular, 1–3 months; ↑ risk age>35, alcohol, rifampicin; monitor LFTs monthly
ValproateMicrovesicular steatosis → hyperammonaemia; risk <3y, polytherapy, metabolic disorders
NevirapineHypersensitivity syndrome (CD4>250 ♀, >400 ♂); rash + hepatitis; HLA-DRB1*01:01
Methotrexate (high-dose)Folic acid rescue; monitor levels at 24h/48h; leucovorin rescue
HalothaneImmune-mediated (trifluoroacetyl hapten); ↓ use now

2. Cholestatic (R ≤ 2)

DrugKey Features
FlucloxacillinCholestatic, 2–4 weeks; HLA-DRB107:01/DQA102:01; prolonged jaundice; elderly >50
Amoxicillin-ClavulanateCholestatic/mixed, 1–6 weeks; ↑ risk >60y, male; avoid re-exposure
AzathioprineCholestatic, nodular regenerative hyperplasia; TPMT testing
ChlorpromazineCholestatic, 2–4 weeks; fever, eosinophilia
OCP (estrogen)Cholestatic, benign; benign hepatic adenoma risk long-term
SulfonamidesMixed/cholestatic, hypersensitivity context

3. Mixed (2 < R < 5)

DrugKey Features
DiclofenacMixed, 1–3 months; ↑ risk elderly, alcohol; avoid in chronic liver disease
AllopurinolDRESS context, mixed pattern; HLA-B*58:01
Phenytoin/CarbamazepineAHS/DRESS, mixed; HLA associations
Anti-TB drugs (INH/RIF/PZA)Combined hepatotoxicity; RIF induces INH metabolism → toxic metabolite

6. 6. 🔬 RUCAM (Roussel Uclaf Causality Assessment Method) — DILI Gold Standard

DomainMax ScoreKey Points
1. Time to onset (5–90d = +2)+2<5d or >90d = -1
2. Course after cessation (↓50% in 8d = +3)+3≥50% in 30d = +2; no info = 0
3. Risk factors (Age≥55, Alcohol, Pregnancy = +1 each)+2Max +2
4. Concomitant drugs (Compatible hepatotoxin = -2)0 to -3None = 0
5. Exclusion of alternatives (All ruled out = +2)+2 to -3<3 ruled out = -2; non-hepatic probable = -3
6. Previous hepatotoxicity (In label = +2)+2Published = +1; none = 0
7. Rechallenge (Positive = +3)+3 to -2Negative = -2; not done = 0

Total: -8 to +14

ScoreCategory
>8Highly probable
6–8Probable
3–5Possible
1–2Unlikely
≤0Excluded

7. 7. 📋 Monitoring & Management Algorithm

⬇ Clinical Pathway
Suspected DILI
↓
**STOP SUSPECT DRUG IMMEDIATELY**
↓
Pattern?
↓
**Hy's Law Check**: ALT>3xULN + Bil>2xULN?
Monitor LFTs weekly Cholestatic: may take months to resolve Ursodeoxycholic acid 15mg/kg for pruritus Exclude obstruction (US/MRCP)
↓
& F & G
Monitor LFTs q48–72h Resolution: ALT halves in 1–2wk Refer if: ALF criteria, no improvement 2wk
↓
**RUCAM Assessment** for causality Document: drug, timeline, pattern, exclusions Report: Yellow Card / FDA MedWatch **NEVER RECHALLENGE**

8. 8. 🎯 FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

ScenarioPatternKey DrugManagement
40yo, paracetamol 15g OD, 6h agoHepatocellularParacetamolNAC IV (Rumack-Matthew), monitor INR, transplant if ALF
60yo, flucloxacillin 3wk, jaundice, ALP 4×ULN, ALT 2×ULNCholestaticFlucloxacillinStop drug; monitor; may persist months; avoid re-exposure
30yo, INH 2mo, ALT 10×ULN, Bil 3×ULNHepatocellular + Hy's LawIsoniazidStop INH; transplant eval; NAC considered
45yo, diclofenac 3mo, ALT 5×ULN, ALP 2×ULNMixedDiclofenacStop NSAID; switch analgesia; monitor
70yo, Augmentin 10d, jaundice, ALP 3×ULNCholestaticCo-amoxiclavStop; avoid re-exposure; monitor
25yo valproate, confused, ammonia 200Microvesicular steatosisValproateStop valproate; L-carnitine; switch AED

9. 9. ❓ Viva Questions (12)

QAnswer
1. Define Hy's Law. What is its prognostic significance?ALT>3×ULN + Bil>2×ULN (no obstruction, no other cause) → 10–50% mortality/transplant; immediate drug cessation
2. Classify DILI patterns by R-value.Hepatocellular R≥5; Cholestatic R≤2; Mixed 2
3. Paracetamol overdose — mechanism, antidote, monitoring?NAPQI (CYP2E1) depletes glutathione → necrosis; NAC (glutathione precursor); Rumack-Matthew nomogram (4–24h); monitor INR, creatinine, glucose, lactate
4. Flucloxacillin DILI — pattern, HLA, time course?Cholestatic; HLA-DRB107:01/DQA102:01; 2–4 weeks; prolonged jaundice; elderly
5. Isoniazid hepatotoxicity — risk factors, monitoring?Age>35, alcohol, rifampicin co-administration; monthly LFTs; symptomatic hepatitis = stop
6. Valproate hepatotoxicity — mechanism, high-risk group, monitoring?Microvesicular steatosis (mitochondrial β-oxidation inhibition); <3y, polytherapy, metabolic disorders; monitor LFTs, ammonia
7. RUCAM domains and scoring?7 domains (Time, Course, Risk factors, Concomitant drugs, Exclusion, Previous toxicity, Rechallenge); -8 to +14; >8 = highly probable
8. Amoxicillin-clavulanate DILI — pattern, risk factors?Cholestatic/mixed; >60y, male; avoid re-exposure
9. Microvesicular vs macrovesicular steatosis?Microvesicular: small droplets, mitochondrial toxicity (valproate, tetracycline, Reye's), hyperammonaemia; Macrovesicular: large droplets, metabolic (NAFLD, alcohol)
10. Anti-TB drug hepatotoxicity — which drug is main culprit? Mechanism?Isoniazid (hydrazine metabolite); Rifampicin induces CYP2E1 → ↑ toxic INH metabolite; PZA adds risk
11. Methotrexate high-dose — rescue, monitoring?Leucovorin (folinic acid) rescue based on 24h/48h MTX levels; monitor LFTs, renal, CBC
12. DILI mimics — what must be excluded (RUCAM Domain 5)?Hepatitis A/B/C/E, CMV, EBV, HSV, autoimmune hepatitis (ANA, ASMA, IgG), Wilson's (ceruloplasmin), α1-antitrypsin, biliary obstruction (US/MRCP), ischaemic hepatitis, alcohol, NAFLD

10. 10. 🤯 Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
Hepatocellular vs CholestaticR-value = (ALT/ULN) ÷ (ALP/ULN); Hepatocellular = ALT predominant; Cholestatic = ALP predominant
Hy's Law = diagnosis?No — prognostic rule; indicates high mortality, not causality
Intrinsic vs IdiosyncraticIntrinsic = dose-dependent, predictable (paracetamol OD); Idiosyncratic = unpredictable, immune/metabolic (flucloxacillin)
Valproate = steatosis type?Microvesicular (mitochondrial β-oxidation inhibition) → hyperammonaemia
RUCAM vs NaranjoRUCAM = DILI-specific, liver-focused domains; Naranjo = general ADR

Mnemonics:

  • "HY'S LAW" = Hepatocellular (ALT>3×) + Yellow (Bil>2×) = High mortality
  • "R-VALUE" = Ratio = ALT/ULN ÷ ALP/ULN; ≥5 = Hepatocellular, ≤2 = Cholestatic, 2–5 = Mixed
  • "INH RISK" = INH hepatotoxicity: Age>35, Lcohol, Rifampicin co-Rx
  • "VALPROATE" = Valproate → Microvesicular steatosis → Hyperammonaemia (not ALT-driven)
  • "FLUCLOX" = Flucloxacillin → Cholestatic → HLA-DRB1*07:01 → Elderly >50
  • "NAC" = N-acetylcysteine for Paracetamol Overdose (and early ALF)

11. 11. 🧠 Mind Map (Mermaid)

DILI
Patterns (R-value)
Hepatocellular (R>=5) --> Paracetamol, INH, Valproate, Nevirapine
Cholestatic (R<=2) --> Flucloxacillin, Augmentin, Azathioprine, Chlorpromazine, OCP
Mixed (2<R<5) --> Diclofenac, Allopurinol, AEDs, Anti-TB
Hy's Law
ALT>3xULN + Bil>2xULN
No obstruction + No other cause
= 10-50% mortality/transplant
Intrinsic vs Idiosyncratic
Intrinsic: Dose-dependent (Paracetamol, MTX)
Idiosyncratic: Unpredictable (Flucloxacillin, INH, Diclofenac)
RUCAM
7 domains, -8 to +14
>8 Highly probable
Monitoring
INH: Monthly LFTs
Valproate: LFTs + Ammonia
MTX HD: Levels 24/48h + Leucovorin
Key Drugs
Paracetamol -> NAC, Rumack-Matthew
Flucloxacillin -> Cholestatic, HLA, Elderly
INH -> Hepatocellular, Hy's Law risk
Valproate -> Microvesicular steatosis, Ammonia
Diclofenac -> Mixed, avoid in liver disease

SectionMaxScore

Target: ≥50/62 (80%)

14. 14. 📝 Exam Answer Modes

1. Long Question (10 marks): "Discuss drug-induced liver injury (DILI) — classification, Hy's Law, and management."

  1. Classification (2): R-value patterns (Hepatocellular R≥5, Cholestatic R≤2, Mixed 2
  2. Hy's Law (2): ALT>3×ULN + Bil>2×ULN, no obstruction/other cause → 10–50% mortality; prognostic not diagnostic
  3. Intrinsic vs Idiosyncratic (2): Dose-dependent vs unpredictable; examples
  4. Key drugs (2): Paracetamol (NAPQI, NAC), INH (monthly LFTs), Flucloxacillin (cholestatic, HLA), Valproate (microvesicular), Anti-TB
  5. Management (2): Stop drug, Hy's Law = transplant referral, RUCAM causality, never rechallenge

2. Short Question (5 marks): "Hy's Law and RUCAM"

  • Hy's Law: ALT>3×ULN + Bil>2×ULN (no obstruction, no other cause) = 10–50% mortality; stop drug immediately
  • RUCAM: 7 domains (-8 to +14); Time, Course, Risk factors, Concomitant drugs, Exclusion of alternatives, Previous hepatotoxicity, Rechallenge; >8 = highly probable

3. Viva (2 min): "60M on flucloxacillin 3 weeks, jaundice, ALP 400, ALT 100, Bil 80. Pattern? Causality? Management?"

  • Pattern: Cholestatic (R = 2.5/3 = 0.8 < 2)
  • Causality: Flucloxacillin classic; RUCAM: Time +2 (3wk), Course +2 (↓), Risk +1 (age), Concomitant 0, Exclusion +2 (viral/autoimmune/biliary ruled out), Previous +2 (in label), Rechallenge 0 = 9 = Highly probable
  • Management: Stop flucloxacillin; monitor LFTs weekly; may persist months; avoid re-exposure; UDCA for pruritus

4. Ward Round (30 sec): "Patient on isoniazid 2 months, ALT 500 (ULN 40), Bil 50 (ULN 20). Action?"

  • Hy's Law criteria MET (ALT>3×, Bil>2×) → STOP INH immediately, transplant referral, NAC considered, monitor INR/encephalopathy

5. Last-Night Revision (1-liners):

  • R = ALT/ULN ÷ ALP/ULN; ≥5 Hepatocellular, ≤2 Cholestatic, 2–5 Mixed
  • Hy's Law = ALT>3× + Bil>2× = 10–50% mortality
  • Paracetamol = NAPQI → NAC (Rumack-Matthew)
  • INH = Hepatocellular, monthly LFTs, Hy's Law risk
  • Flucloxacillin = Cholestatic, HLA-DRB1*07:01, elderly
  • Valproate = Microvesicular steatosis → hyperammonaemia
  • Augmentin = Cholestatic, >60y male
  • RUCAM = DILI gold standard; 7 domains, >8 highly probable
  • NEVER rechallenge idiosyncratic DILI

15. 15. 📚 Summary Card

DILI TRIAD FOR EXAMS:
1. PATTERN: R-value → Hepatocellular (INH, Paracetamol) / Cholestatic (Flucloxacillin, Augmentin) / Mixed (Diclofenac)
2. HY'S LAW: ALT>3× + Bil>2× = STOP DRUG, TRANSPLANT REFERRAL
3. KEY DRUGS: Paracetamol (NAC), INH (monthly LFTs), Flucloxacillin (HLA, elderly), Valproate (ammonia), Anti-TB (INH+RIF interaction)
RUCAM = DILI causality gold standard; >8 = highly probable
NEVER RECHALLENGE idiosyncratic DILI

16. 16. ❓ MCQs (15)

  1. R-value in DILI is calculated as:

A. ALP/ULN ÷ ALT/ULN

B. ALT/ULN ÷ ALP/ULN ✓

C. ALT × ALP / ULN²

D. (ALT + ALP) / 2×ULN

E. ALT/ULN - ALP/ULN

  1. Hepatocellular pattern DILI corresponds to R-value:

A. ≤2

B. 2–5

C. ≥5 ✓

D. <1

E. Any value

  1. Hy's Law criteria include all EXCEPT:

A. ALT >3×ULN

B. Bilirubin >2×ULN

C. No alkaline phosphatase elevation ✓

D. No biliary obstruction

E. No other identifiable cause

  1. Paracetamol hepatotoxicity mechanism:

A. Direct mitochondrial toxicity

B. NAPQI formation via CYP2E1 depleting glutathione ✓

C. Immune-mediated hypersensitivity

D. Biliary obstruction

E. Microvesicular steatosis

  1. Flucloxacillin DILI is typically:

A. Hepatocellular

B. Cholestatic ✓

C. Mixed

D. Microvesicular steatosis

E. Acute liver failure

  1. Valproate-induced hepatotoxicity is characterised by:

A. Macrovesicular steatosis

B. Microvesicular steatosis with hyperammonaemia ✓

C. Cholestatic picture

D. Hy's Law criteria

E. Eosinophilia

  1. Isoniazid hepatotoxicity risk factors:

A. Age <35, no alcohol

B. Age >35, alcohol, rifampicin co-administration ✓

C. Paediatric age only

D. Renal impairment

D. Pregnancy

  1. RUCAM total score range:

A. 0–10

B. -5 to +10

C. -8 to +14 ✓

D. 1–20

E. -10 to +10

  1. RUCAM category for score of 9:

A. Probable

B. Highly probable ✓

C. Possible

D. Unlikely

E. Excluded

  1. Amoxicillin-clavulanate DILI typical pattern and risk group:

A. Hepatocellular, young females

B. Cholestatic/mixed, elderly males >60 ✓

C. Mixed, children

D. Hepatocellular, alcoholics

E. Cholestatic, pregnant women

  1. Microvesicular vs macrovesicular steatosis — valproate causes:

A. Microvesicular ✓

B. Macrovesicular

C. Both equally

D. Neither

E. Cholestasis

  1. Anti-TB regimen — rifampicin increases isoniazid hepatotoxicity by:

A. Inhibiting INH metabolism

B. Inducing CYP2E1 → ↑ toxic hydrazine metabolite ✓

C. Protein binding displacement

D. Reducing INH clearance

E. No interaction

  1. Methotrexate high-dose rescue:

A. N-acetylcysteine

B. Leucovorin (folinic acid) ✓

C. Vitamin B12

D. Thymidine

E. Glutathione

  1. DILI management — first step always:

A. Start steroids

B. STOP SUSPECT DRUG ✓

C. IV NAC

D. Liver biopsy

E. Transplant listing

  1. Never rechallenge applies to which DILI type?

A. Intrinsic (dose-dependent)

B. Idiosyncratic ✓

C. Both

D. Neither

E. Only Hy's Law cases

FrontBack
R-value formula(ALT/ULN) ÷ (ALP/ULN)
Hepatocellular R≥5
Cholestatic R≤2
Mixed R2–5
Hy's LawALT>3×ULN + Bil>2×ULN (no obstruction, no other cause) = 10–50% mortality
