– Abx C.diff/superinfection
High-Risk NTI Drugs
Warfarin (INR)
Digoxin (level, K, Mg)
Lithium (level, renal)
Phenytoin (level, free)
Aminoglycosides (trough)
Vancomycin (AUC)
Immunosuppressants (trough)
Prevention
Start low, go slow
TDM
Renal/hepatic adjust
Interaction screen

11. 11. 🧪 Self-Test Scorecard

SectionMaxScore
Definition & comparison Type A vs B6
Mechanisms (3 categories + examples)12
NTI drugs & monitoring10
Prevention strategies6
Viva answers8
Total42

Target: ≥34/42 (80%)

12. 12. 📝 Exam Answer Modes

1. Long Question (10 marks): "Classify ADRs with examples. Discuss Type A ADRs in detail."

  1. Classification (2): Rawlins/Thompson → Type A (Augmented), Type B (Bizarre), Type C (Chronic), D (Delayed), E (End of use), F (Failure) — or DoTS
  2. Type A Definition (2): Dose-dependent, predictable, common, low mortality
  3. Three Mechanisms (3): Excessive therapeutic, Unwanted pharmacological, Secondary pharmacological — with 2 examples each
  4. High-risk NTI drugs (2): Table of drug, monitoring, target, ADR
  5. Prevention (1): Start low/go slow, TDM, dose adjust, interaction screen, deprescribing

2. Short Question (5 marks): "Type A vs Type B ADR"

  • Table: Mechanism, Dose-dependence, Predictability, Incidence, Mortality, Examples, Management

3. Viva (2 min): "80yo on warfarin, INR 5.8, no bleed. Why Type A? Manage."

  • Type A: Excessive anticoagulation (dose-dependent, predictable)
  • Manage: Omit 1–2 doses; Vit K 1mg PO if high bleed risk/elderly; Restart lower dose; Review interacting drugs

4. Ward Round (30 sec): "Patient on furosemide 80mg, K⁺ 2.9. Type A or B?"

  • Type A — Unwanted pharmacological effect (renal K⁺ wasting). Action: K⁺ replacement, ↓ dose, add K⁺-sparing, review indication.

5. Last-Night Revision (1-liners):

  • Type A = Augmented = Dose-dependent = Predictable = Common
  • Type B = Bizarre = Idiosyncratic = Unpredictable = Rare = High mortality
  • NTI drugs: Warfarin, Digoxin, Lithium, Phenytoin, Gentamicin, Vancomycin, Tacrolimus, Theophylline
  • Mechanisms: 1° Excessive therapeutic 2° Off-target 3° Secondary cascade

13. 13. 📚 Summary Card

TYPE A ADR TRIAD:
1. Excessive therapeutic — Warfarin bleed, Insulin hypo, Digoxin toxicity
2. Unwanted pharmacological — NSAID GI/renal, TCA anticholinergic, ACEi cough, Beta-agonist tremor
3. Secondary pharmacological — Steroid metabolic/bone, Diuretic electrolytes, Abx C.diff
PREVENTION: Start low, go slow + TDM (NTI) + Renal/hepatic adjust + Interaction screen + Deprescribe

14. 14. ❓ MCQs (10)

  1. Which characteristic defines a Type A (Augmented) ADR?

A. Idiosyncratic, unpredictable

B. Dose-dependent, predictable from pharmacology ✓

C. Occurs after long-term use only

D. Immunologically mediated

E. Genetic predisposition required

  1. Warfarin-induced haemorrhage with INR >4 is an example of which Type A mechanism?

A. Unwanted pharmacological effect

B. Excessive therapeutic effect ✓

C. Secondary pharmacological effect

D. Idiosyncratic reaction

E. Drug interaction effect

  1. Amitriptyline causing dry mouth, constipation, and urinary retention represents:

A. Type B ADR

B. Type A: Unwanted pharmacological effect (anticholinergic) ✓

C. Excessive therapeutic effect

D. Secondary pharmacological effect

E. Drug interaction

  1. ACE inhibitor-induced dry cough is mediated by:

A. Excessive therapeutic effect (oversuppression of Ang II)

B. Bradykinin accumulation (secondary pharmacological effect) ✓

C. Unwanted pharmacological effect (off-target receptor)

D. Direct bronchial irritation

E. Type B idiosyncratic reaction

  1. Which drug does NOT have a narrow therapeutic index requiring TDM?

A. Digoxin

B. Lithium

C. Phenytoin

D. Amoxicillin ✓

E. Warfarin

  1. An elderly patient on furosemide 80 mg daily develops hyponatraemia (Na⁺ 125 mmol/L). This is a Type A ADR via which mechanism?

A. Excessive therapeutic effect

B. Unwanted pharmacological effect (impaired water excretion) ✓

C. Secondary pharmacological effect

D. Type B idiosyncratic

E. Drug interaction

  1. Salbutamol-induced tremor and hypokalaemia are due to:

A. Excessive beta-2 bronchodilation

B. Unwanted pharmacological effect (beta-2 stimulation in muscle, beta-1 in heart) ✓

C. Secondary effect of hypoxia correction

D. Type B hypersensitivity

E. Drug interaction with steroids

  1. Corticosteroid-induced osteoporosis is classified as:

A. Type A: Excessive therapeutic effect

B. Type A: Unwanted pharmacological effect

C. Type A: Secondary pharmacological effect (metabolic/bone) ✓

D. Type C: Chronic ADR only

E. Type B: Idiosyncratic

  1. Key prevention strategy for Type A ADRs in elderly polypharmacy patients:

A. Avoid all medications

B. Start low, go slow; regular review; deprescribing ✓

C. Use only Type B drugs

D. Increase doses gradually to tolerance

E. Routine genetic testing

  1. Patient on digoxin 125 mcg develops nausea, yellow vision, and bradycardia. Serum K⁺ 3.1. Most likely explanation:

A. Type B ADR

B. Type A ADR: Digoxin toxicity precipitated by hypokalaemia ✓

C. Drug interaction with ACEi

D. Secondary pharmacological effect

E. Idiopathic intolerance

15. 15. 🃏 Flashcards (Anki-ready)

FrontBack
Type A ADR definitionDose-dependent, predictable from pharmacology, common, usually reversible
Type B ADR definitionIdiosyncratic, non-dose-dependent, unpredictable, rare, high mortality
Type A mechanism 1Excessive therapeutic effect — Warfarin bleed, Insulin hypo, Digoxin toxicity
Type A mechanism 2Unwanted pharmacological (off-target) — NSAID COX-1 GI/renal, TCA anticholinergic, ACEi cough, Beta-agonist tremor
Type A mechanism 3Secondary pharmacological — Steroid metabolic/bone, Diuretic electrolytes, Abx C.diff
NTI drugs requiring TDMWarfarin (INR), Digoxin (level/K/Mg), Lithium (level/renal), Phenytoin (level), Gentamicin (trough), Vancomycin (AUC), Tacrolimus/Ciclosporin (trough), Theophylline (level)
ACEi cough mechanismBradykinin accumulation (ACE degrades bradykinin)
NSAID GI/renal mechanismCOX-1 inhibition → ↓ prostaglandins → ↓ gastric mucus/HCO₃⁻, ↓ renal vasodilation
TCA anticholinergic SEsDry mouth, constipation, urinary retention, confusion, blurred vision, tachycardia

16. 16. ✅ Answer Keys

1. MCQs

  1. B — Type A = dose-dependent, predictable
  2. B — Warfarin bleed = excessive anticoagulation (therapeutic effect)
  3. B — TCA anticholinergic = unwanted off-target muscarinic blockade
  4. B — Bradykinin accumulation = secondary pharmacological effect
  5. D — Amoxicillin wide TI, no TDM
  6. B — Furosemide hyponatraemia = unwanted effect (impaired dilution)
  7. B — Salbutamol tremor/hypoK = beta-2 off-target (muscle, K⁺ shift)
  8. C — Steroid osteoporosis = secondary metabolic effect (chronological but pharmacologically mediated)
  9. B — Start low/go slow + review + deprescribe
  10. B — Digoxin toxicity + hypokalaemia = Type A (predictable, dose/exposure dependent)

File: /mnt/tb/Medicine/Clinical Therapeutics and Good Prescribing/ADRs/Type A (Augmented).md | Status: draft → upgrade to full-fcps-mrcp-note after review

▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 2.1.16 Type B Bizarre

Status: draft | Chapter: 2 — Clinical Therapeutics and Good Prescribing | Heading: Adverse Drug Reactions | Exam Priority: ⭐⭐⭐ HIGH (Idiosyncratic, high mortality, VIVA favourites — SJS/TEN, DRESS, AHS, Torsades)

1. 1. 🎯 Learning Objectives

  • [ ] Define Type B ADR: idiosyncratic, non-dose-dependent, unpredictable
  • [ ] Classify Type B by mechanism: immunological (hypersensitivity I–IV), non-immunological idiosyncrasy
  • [ ] Recognise high-mortality syndromes: SJS/TEN, DRESS, AGEP, AHS, Torsades, Anaphylaxis
  • [ ] Apply causality assessment (Naranjo, WHO-UMC, RUCAM) to Type B reactions
  • [ ] Know absolute contraindication to rechallenge

2. 2. 📖 Definition & Core Features

FeatureType B (Bizarre)
SynonymIdiosyncratic
Dose-dependenceNo (may need threshold but not linear)
PredictabilityUnpredictable from pharmacology
IncidenceRare (1:1,000 – 1:100,000)
MortalityHigh (can be fatal)
MechanismImmunological (hypersensitivity) or non-immunological idiosyncrasy
RechallengeAbsolutely contraindicated
Genetic predispositionOften (HLA alleles, enzyme polymorphisms)

3. 3. 🧬 Classification by Mechanism

1. A. Immunological (Hypersensitivity Reactions — Gell & Coombs)

TypeMechanismMediatorsOnsetClassic Drug ExamplesClinical Syndrome
Type I (Immediate)IgE-mediated mast cell/basophil degranulationHistamine, tryptase, leukotrienes, PGD₂Minutes–hoursPenicillins, cephalosporins, NSAIDs, muscle relaxants, insulin, latexAnaphylaxis, urticaria, angioedema, bronchospasm, anaphylactoid
Type II (Cytotoxic)IgG/IgM + complement → cell lysisComplement, ADCCDays–weeksPenicillins, cephalosporins (haemolytic anaemia); Methyldopa (AIHA); Quinidine (thrombocytopenia); Heparin (HIT)Immune haemolytic anaemia, thrombocytopenia, neutropenia
Type III (Immune Complex)IgG/IgM-antigen complexes → vasculitisComplement, neutrophils1–3 weeksPenicillins, sulfonamides, allopurinol, biologics (infliximab), vaccinesSerum sickness, vasculitis, glomerulonephritis, drug fever
Type IV (Delayed T-cell)T-cell mediated (CD4+/CD8+)Cytokines (IFN-γ, TNF-α, IL-5)Days–weeksAllopurinol, sulfonamides, antiepileptics, abacavir, dapsone, NSAIDsMorbilliform, SJS/TEN, DRESS, AGEP, AHS, maculopapular exanthema (MPE)
Subtypes of Type IV (PHIL):
- IVa (CD4+ Th1): Maculopapular, DRESS
- IVb (CD4+ Th2): Eosinophilic (DRESS, AHS)
- IVc (CD8+ cytotoxic): SJS/TEN, AGEP, MPE — granulysin, perforin, Fas-FasL
- IVd (T-cell → neutrophil): AGEP — IL-8, CXCL8

2. B. Non-Immunological Idiosyncrasy

MechanismExampleSyndrome
Enzyme deficiency (pharmacogenetic)G6PD deficiency + oxidative drugs (primaquine, dapsone, sulfonamides)Haemolytic anaemia
CYP2D6 poor metaboliser + codeineNo analgesia
CYP2D6 ultra-rapid + codeineToxic morphine levels → apnoea
NAT2 slow acetylator + isoniazidPeripheral neuropathy, hepatotoxicity
TPMT deficiency + azathioprine/6-MPMyelosuppression
Mitochondrial toxicityValproate, linezolid, NRTIs (zidovudine)Hepatic steatosis, lactic acidosis, neuropathy
ChannelopathyQT-prolonging drugs in congenital LQTSTorsades de Pointes
Idiosyncratic hepatotoxicityIsoniazid, valproate, diclofenac, amoxicillin-clavulanateDILI (Hy's Law)

4. 4. ⚠️ High-Mortality Type B Syndromes (Must Know for Exams)

1. 1. Stevens-Johnson Syndrome / Toxic Epidermal Necrolysis (SJS/TEN)

FeatureSJSTENOverlap (SJS/TEN)
BSA detachment<10%>30%10–30%
Mucosal sites≥2≥2≥2
Typical drugsAllopurinol, sulfonamides, AEDs (CZP, PHT, LMG, OXC), NSAIDs, nevirapineSameSame
HLA associationsB15:02 (CZP/PHT/OXC), B58:01 (Allopurinol), A*31:01 (CZP DRESS)
Mortality~5–10%~30–50%~15–25%
SCORTEN7 factors: age>40, HR>120, cancer, BSA>10%, BUN>28, Glc>14, HCO₃<20

SCORTEN Mortality Prediction:

ScoreMortality
0–13%
212%
335%
458%
590%+

Management: Stop culprit immediately → ICU/burn unit → supportive (fluids, electrolytes, wound care, infection prevention) → IVIG (controversial), cyclosporine, etanercept (emerging) → NO systemic steroids (controversial, ↑ sepsis risk)

2. 2. DRESS (Drug Reaction with Eosinophilia and Systemic Symptoms)

FeatureDetails
TriadFever + Rash + Organ involvement
Typical onset2–8 weeks after drug start (delayed)
Key drugsAllopurinol, AEDs (CZP, PHT, PB, LMG, VPA), sulfonamides, dapsone, abacavir, minocycline, allopurinol
HLAB58:01 (Allopurinol), B15:02 (CZP), A31:01 (CZP), B13:01 (Dapsone)
RegiSCAR criteriaFever ≥38°C, lymphadenopathy, eosinophilia >1.5×10⁹/L or atypical lymphs, organ involvement (liver, kidney, lung, heart), rash, reaction >2wks
Mortality~10% (liver failure, myocarditis)
ManagementStop drug → systemic steroids (pred 1mg/kg) → slow taper 6–12wks → monitor LFTs, renal, cardiac

3. 3. AGEP (Acute Generalised Exanthematous Pustulosis)

FeatureDetails
PresentationAcute febrile + non-follicular pustules on oedematous erythema
Onset1–5 days (rapid)
Key drugsBeta-lactams (esp. aminopenicillins), quinolones, hydroxychloroquine, diltiazem, sulfonamides
HistologySubcorneal pustules, neutrophilic infiltration
BiomarkerNeutrophilia (not eosinophilia)
CourseSelf-limiting <2 weeks after withdrawal
MortalityLow (<5%)

4. 4. AHS (Anticonvulsant Hypersensitivity Syndrome) / DRESS by AEDs

FeatureDetails
DrugsPhenytoin, carbamazepine, phenobarbital, lamotrigine, valproate
TriadFever, rash, systemic involvement (hepatitis, nephritis, pneumonitis)
Onset1–8 weeks
HLAHLA-B15:02 (CZP), HLA-A31:01 (CZP DRESS), HLA-B*38:01 (dapsone/CZP)
Cross-reactivityHigh among aromatic AEDs (CZP, PHT, PB, OXC) — avoid all if reaction to one
ManagementStop all aromatic AEDs → switch to non-aromatic (levetiracetam, topiramate, gabapentin, lacosamide, zonisamide)

5. 5. Torsades de Pointes (Drug-Induced)

FeatureDetails
MechanismhERG (IKr) blockade → prolonged repolarisation → afterdepolarisations → R-on-T
Risk factorsFemale, elderly, hypokalaemia, hypomagnesaemia, bradycardia, congenital LQTS, polypharmacy (multiple QT drugs)
High-risk drugsAntiarrhythmics (Class Ia, III), antipsychotics (haloperidol, thioridazine, ziprasidone), antidepressants (citalopram, escitalopram, TCAs), antibiotics (macrolides, fluoroquinolones), antiemetics (ondansetron, domperidone), antifungals (azole), methadone
MonitoringBaseline ECG, repeat if risk factors; QTc >500ms or Δ>60ms = high risk
Acute managementIV Magnesium sulfate 2g bolus (even if normal Mg²⁺) → correct K⁺/Mg²⁺ → pacing/isoprenaline if paused → stop offending drug

6. 6. Anaphylaxis (Type I)

FeatureDetails
CriteriaAcute onset + skin/mucosal + respiratory compromise OR hypotension OR GI symptoms
Common drugsPenicillins, cephalosporins, NSAIDs, neuromuscular blockers, radiocontrast, insulin, biologics
ManagementIM Adrenaline 1:1000 (0.5mg = 0.5mL) anterolateral thigh → supine + legs raised → O₂ → IV fluids → chlorphenamine + hydrocortisone (adjuncts) → observe 6–12h
TryptasePeak 1–2h, half-life 2h — sample at 1–2h and 24h (baseline)

7. 7. DILI (Drug-Induced Liver Injury) — Idiosyncratic (Type B)

PatternDrugsFeatures
Hepatocellular (ALT>ALP)Isoniazid, valproate, paracetamol (overdose = Type A), nevirapine, flucloxacillinHy's Law: ALT>3×ULN + Bilirubin>2×ULN → 10–50% mortality
Cholestatic (ALP>ALT)Flucloxacillin, amoxicillin-clavulanate, azathioprine, chlorpromazine, anabolic steroidsPruritus, jaundice, prolonged
MixedDiclofenac, sulfonamides, allopurinolIntermediate
HLADRB107:01/DQA102:01 (Flucloxacillin), B*57:01 (Flucloxacillin in some)

5. 5. 🎯 FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

SyndromeKey Drug(s)OnsetKey FeatureMortalityManagement
AnaphylaxisPenicillins, NSAIDs, NM blockersMinutes–hrsHypotension, angioedema, wheezeHigh if delayedIM Adrenaline 0.5mg
SJS/TENAllopurinol, Sulfa, AEDs, NSAIDs1–4 weeksBSA detachment, mucosal erosions5–50%Stop drug, ICU, supportive
DRESSAllopurinol, AEDs, Dapsone, Abacavir2–8 weeksFever, eosinophilia, organ involvement~10%Stop drug, steroids 1mg/kg, slow taper
AGEPBeta-lactams, quinolones1–5 daysNon-follicular pustules, neutrophiliaLowStop drug, supportive
TorsadesQT-prolonging drugs + risk factorsVariablePolymorphic VT, R-on-THighIV MgSO₄ 2g, correct K/Mg, pacing
DILI (Hy's Law)INH, Valproate, Flucloxacillin, AugmentinVariableALT>3×ULN + Bil>2×ULN10–50%Stop drug, monitor, transplant eval

6. 6. ❓ Viva Questions (12)

QExpected Answer
1. Define Type B ADR. How does it differ from Type A?Idiosyncratic, non-dose-dependent, unpredictable, rare, high mortality. Type A: dose-dependent, predictable, common, lower mortality.
2. Classify Type B by mechanism.Immunological (Gell-Coombs I–IV) + Non-immunological (pharmacogenetic, mitochondrial, channelopathy)
3. SJS vs TEN vs Overlap — BSA criteria?SJS <10%, Overlap 10–30%, TEN >30% BSA detachment; all ≥2 mucosal sites
4. SCORTEN components? Mortality for score 3?Age>40, HR>120, malignancy, BSA>10%, BUN>28, Glc>14, HCO₃<20. Score 3 = 35% mortality
5. DRESS: typical onset, key labs, management?2–8 weeks; eosinophilia >1.5, atypical lymphs, LFTs, renal; stop drug, pred 1mg/kg, taper 6–12wks
6. AGEP vs SJS/TEN: distinguishing features?AGEP: pustules (not bullae), neutrophilia (not eosinophilia), onset 1–5d, self-limiting <2wks; SJS/TEN: bullae, necrosis, eosinophilia, high mortality
7. Aromatic AED cross-reactivity? Non-aromatic alternatives?High cross-reactivity (CZP, PHT, PB, OXC, LMG). Non-aromatic: Levetiracetam, Topiramate, Gabapentin, Lacosamide, Zonisamide, Pregabalin
8. Torsades: mechanism, highest-risk drug classes, acute treatment?hERG (IKr) blockade; Class Ia/III antiarrhythmics, antipsychotics, macrolides, fluoroquinolones, ondansetron, methadone; IV MgSO₄ 2g bolus
9. Hy's Law for DILI? Significance?ALT>3×ULN + Bilirubin>2×ULN (no obstruction) → predicts 10–50% mortality / transplant; stop drug immediately
10. Flucloxacillin cholestatic DILI: HLA association?HLA-DRB107:01 / DQA102:01 (Class II); onset 2–4 weeks; prolonged jaundice
11. Abacavir hypersensitivity: HLA? PPV/NPV? Management if occurs?HLA-B*57:01; NPV 98–100%, PPV ~50%; Stop permanently, never rechallenge, document allergy
12. Allopurinol SJS/TEN: HLA? Screening in whom? Alternative?HLA-B*58:01; Screen Han Chinese, Korean, Thai, African, CKD≥3; Alternative: Febuxostat (CARE: CV risk)

7. 7. 🤯 Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
Type B vs Type C/D/E/FType B = idiosyncratic (unpredictable); Type C = chronic (time-dependent); D = delayed (carcinogenesis); E = end of use (withdrawal); F = failure (inefficacy)
SJS/TEN vs DRESS vs AGEPSJS/TEN: necrosis/bullae, mucosal, high mortality; DRESS: eosinophilia, organ involvement, delayed onset; AGEP: pustules, neutrophilia, rapid onset, benign
AHS vs DRESSAHS = DRESS caused specifically by aromatic AEDs — same spectrum; cross-reactivity among aromatic AEDs
Torsades = Type A or B?Type B — idiosyncratic (not all get it at same dose); requires susceptibility (congenital LQTS, electrolytes, polypharmacy)
Anaphylaxis vs AnaphylactoidAnaphylaxis = IgE-mediated (Type I); Anaphylactoid = non-IgE, direct mast cell activation (e.g., radiocontrast, opioids, vancomycin "red man") — treatment identical

Mnemonics:

  • "B = Bizarre = Bad (high mortality) = idiosyncratic"
  • "SCORTEN" = Skin >10%, Cancer, Old >40, Renal (BUN>28), Tachy >120, Electrolytes (HCO₃<20, Glc>14), Neoplasia
  • "REGISCAR for DRESS" = Rash, Eosinophilia, Globular (atypical lymphs), Intermittent fever, Systemic organ, Cytology, Allopurinol/AEDs, Reaction >2wks
  • "Hy's Law" = Hepatocellular (ALT>3×) + Yellow (Bil>2×) = High mortality
  • "IV Mg for Torsades" = Magnesium Mg for Mechanism (hERG block)

8. 8. 🧠 Mind Map (Mermaid)

Type B ADR
Immunological (Gell-Coombs)
Type I (IgE) --> Anaphylaxis, Urticaria
Type II (Cytotoxic) --> AIHA, ITP, Neutropenia, HIT
Type III (Immune Complex) --> Serum Sickness, Vasculitis
Type IV (T-cell)
– IVa (Th1) --> Maculopapular, DRESS
– IVb (Th2) --> Eosinophilic DRESS, AHS
– IVc (CD8 Cytotoxic) --> SJS/TEN, AGEP, MPE
– IVd (Neutrophil) --> AGEP
Non-Immunological
Pharmacogenetic
– G6PD deficiency --> Oxidative drugs haemolysis
– CYP2D6 UM --> Codeine toxicity
– TPMT deficiency --> Azathioprine myelosuppression
– NAT2 slow --> INH neuropathy/hepatotox
Mitochondrial toxicity
– Valproate, NRTIs, Linezolid
Channelopathy
– Congenital LQTS + QT drugs --> Torsades
Idiosyncratic DILI
– INH, Valproate, Flucloxacillin, Augmentin
High-Mortality Syndromes
Anaphylaxis
SJS/TEN (SCORTEN)
DRESS (RegiSCAR)
AGEP
Torsades (hERG)
DILI (Hy's Law)
HLA Associations
B*15:02 --> CZP/PHT/OXC SJS/TEN
B*58:01 --> Allopurinol SCAR
B*57:01 --> Abacavir HSS
A*31:01 --> CZP DRESS
DRB1*07:01 --> Flucloxacillin DILI
B*13:01 --> Dapsone HSS

10. 10. 🧪 Self-Test Scorecard

SectionMaxScore

Target: ≥56/70 (80%)

11. 11. 📝 Exam Answer Modes

1. Long Question (10 marks): "Discuss the classification, clinical features, and management of severe cutaneous adverse reactions (SCARs)."

  1. Classification (2): Type B (idiosyncratic), Type IV T-cell mediated; SCARs = SJS/TEN, DRESS, AGEP, AHS
  2. SJS/TEN (3): Definition (BSA), SCORTEN, drugs (allopurinol, sulfa, AEDs), HLA (B15:02, B58:01), management (stop drug, ICU, supportive, IVIG/cyclosporine/etanercept, NO steroids)
  3. DRESS (2): RegiSCAR criteria, 2–8wk onset, eosinophilia/organ involvement, drugs, HLA, steroids 1mg/kg taper 6–12wks
  4. AGEP (2): Pustules, neutrophilia, 1–5d onset, beta-lactams, self-limiting
  5. Cross-reactivity (1): Aromatic AEDs — avoid all if one causes SCAR

2. Short Question (5 marks): "SJS vs TEN vs DRESS — distinguish."

FeatureSJSTENDRESS
BSA detachment<10%>30%Variable (usually <10%)
Mucosal≥2 sites≥2 sitesOften
Onset1–4 wks1–4 wks2–8 wks
Key labs——Eosinophilia, atypical lymphs
Mortality5–10%30–50%~10%
HLAB*15:02B*15:02B58:01, A31:01

3. Viva (2 min): "Patient on allopurinol 2 weeks, fever, rash, eosinophilia 3×10⁹, ALT 200. Diagnosis? Management?"

  • DRESS (RegiSCAR criteria met)
  • Stop allopurinol immediately
  • Prednisolone 1mg/kg, slow taper 6–12 weeks
  • Monitor LFTs, renal, cardiac
  • Screen HLA-B*58:01 (retrospective)
  • Alternative: Febuxostat (caution CV risk)

4. Ward Round (30 sec): "Patient on clarithromycin + citalopram develops syncope. ECG shows polymorphic VT, twisting points. Immediate management?"

  • Torsades de Pointes — IV Magnesium sulfate 2g bolus (even if Mg²⁺ normal)
  • Stop clarithromycin + citalopram (both QT prolonging)
  • Correct K⁺ >4.5, Mg²⁺ >1.0
  • Temporary pacing / isoprenaline if recurrent

5. Last-Night Revision (1-liners):

  • Type B = Bizarre = Idiosyncratic = Unpredictable = High mortality
  • SJS <10%, TEN >30%, Overlap 10–30% BSA
  • SCORTEN: 7 factors, score 3 = 35% mortality
  • DRESS: 2–8 wks, eosinophilia, organ involvement, steroids taper 6–12wks
  • AGEP: pustules, neutrophilia, 1–5d, beta-lactams, benign
  • Torsades: hERG block → IV Mg 2g
  • Hy's Law: ALT>3× + Bil>2× = 10–50% mortality
  • HLA: B15:02 (CZP), B58:01 (Allo), B57:01 (ABC), A31:01 (CZP DRESS), DRB1*07:01 (Fluclox)
  • Aromatic AEDs cross-react: CZP, PHT, PB, OXC, LMG → switch to LEV, TPM, GBP, LCM

12. 12. 📚 Summary Card

SCAR TRIAD FOR EXAMS:
1. SJS/TEN — Necrosis/bullae, SCORTEN, HLA-B15:02/B58:01, ICU, stop drug, no steroids
2. DRESS — Eosinophilia + organ, 2–8wks, HLA-B58:01/A31:01, Steroids 1mg/kg taper 6–12wks
3. AGEP — Pustules + neutrophilia, 1–5d, beta-lactams, benign, self-limiting
Torsades = hERG block → IV MgSO₄ 2g
Anaphylaxis = IM Adrenaline 0.5mg
Hy's Law = ALT>3× + Bil>2× = transplant/mortality risk

13. 13. ❓ MCQs (15)

  1. Which statement BEST describes a Type B (Bizarre) ADR?

A. Dose-dependent and predictable

B. Idiosyncratic, non-dose-dependent, unpredictable, high mortality ✓

C. Occurs only after chronic use

D. Mediated by IgG antibodies only

E. Preventable by therapeutic drug monitoring

  1. Gell-Coombs Type IV hypersensitivity is mediated by:

A. IgE

B. IgG/IgM + complement

C. Immune complexes

D. T-lymphocytes (CD4+ and CD8+) ✓

E. Neutrophils

  1. SJS/TEN overlap syndrome is defined by BSA detachment of:

A. <10%

B. 10–30% ✓

C. >30%

D. 30–50%

E. >50%

  1. A patient on allopurinol for 3 weeks develops fever, diffuse maculopapular rash, facial oedema, eosinophilia 2.5×10⁹/L, and ALT 300 U/L. Most likely diagnosis?

A. SJS

B. TEN

C. DRESS ✓

D. AGEP

E. Serum sickness

  1. SCORTEN includes all EXCEPT:

A. Age >40

B. Heart rate >120

C. Serum creatinine >200 ✓

D. BUN >28 mg/dL

E. Bicarbonate <20 mmol/L

F. Glucose >14 mmol/L

G. BSA detached >10%

  1. AGEP is characterised by:

A. Full-thickness epidermal necrosis

B. Non-follicular pustules on oedematous erythema + neutrophilia ✓

C. Eosinophilia >1.5×10⁹/L

D. Onset 4–8 weeks after drug initiation

E. Mucosal involvement in >90%

  1. Which drug class has the HIGHEST cross-reactivity in aromatic antiepileptic hypersensitivity?

A. Non-aromatic AEDs (levetiracetam, topiramate)

B. Aromatic AEDs (carbamazepine, phenytoin, phenobarbital, oxcarbazepine, lamotrigine) ✓

C. Valproate only

D. Benzodiazepines

E. Gabapentinoids

  1. Torsades de Pointes is caused by blockade of which potassium channel?

A. IKs

B. IK1

C. hERG (IKr) ✓

D. Ito

E. IKATP

  1. Acute management of Torsades de Pointes (regardless of magnesium level):

A. IV Amiodarone 300mg

B. IV Magnesium sulfate 2g bolus ✓

C. IV Lidocaine 1mg/kg

D. Synchronised cardioversion 100J

E. IV Potassium chloride 20mmol

  1. Hy's Law for DILI predicts:

A. Mild self-limiting hepatitis

B. 10–50% mortality or need for transplant ✓

C. Cholestatic pattern only

D. Resolution within 2 weeks

E. No long-term sequelae

  1. Flucloxacillin-induced cholestatic DILI is associated with:

A. HLA-B*15:02

B. HLA-B*58:01

C. HLA-B*57:01

D. HLA-DRB107:01 / DQA102:01 ✓

E. HLA-A*31:01

  1. Abacavir hypersensitivity syndrome: HLA allele and management if reaction occurs?

A. HLA-B*15:02; reduce dose

B. HLA-B*57:01; stop permanently, never rechallenge ✓

C. HLA-B*58:01; switch to alternative NRTI

D. HLA-A*31:01; continue with steroids

E. HLA-DRB1*07:01; monitor only

  1. Valproate-induced hepatotoxicity is primarily due to:

A. IgE-mediated hypersensitivity

B. Mitochondrial toxicity (beta-oxidation inhibition) ✓

C. CYP2C9 inhibition

D. Immune complex deposition

E. Direct dose-dependent toxicity (Type A)

  1. A patient on carbamazepine develops SJS. Which HLA allele is most strongly associated?

A. HLA-A*31:01

B. HLA-B*15:02 ✓

C. HLA-B*58:01

D. HLA-B*57:01

E. HLA-DRB1*07:01

  1. DRESS vs AGEP — key distinguishing lab finding:

A. Eosinophilia (DRESS) vs Neutrophilia (AGEP) ✓

B. Lymphopenia (DRESS) vs Leukocytosis (AGEP)

C. Thrombocytopenia (DRESS) vs Thrombocytosis (AGEP)

D. Both have eosinophilia

E. Both have neutrophilia

14. 14. 🃏 Flashcards (Anki-ready)

FrontBack
Type B ADR definitionIdiosyncratic, non-dose-dependent, unpredictable, rare, high mortality
Gell-Coombs Type IIgE-mediated, minutes-hours: Anaphylaxis, urticaria
Gell-Coombs Type IIIgG/IgM cytotoxic: AIHA, ITP, HIT
Gell-Coombs Type IIIImmune complexes: Serum sickness, vasculitis
Gell-Coombs Type IVT-cell mediated: SJS/TEN, DRESS, AGEP, MPE, AHS
Type IVc (CD8 cytotoxic)SJS/TEN, AGEP, MPE — granulysin, perforin, Fas-FasL
Type IVd (neutrophil)AGEP — IL-8, CXCL8
SJS BSA<10%
TEN BSA>30%
Overlap BSA10–30%
SCORTEN 7 factorsAge>40, HR>120, Cancer, BSA>10%, BUN>28, Glc>14, HCO3<20
SCORTEN score 3 mortality35%
DRESS onset2–8 weeks
DRESS key labsEosinophilia >1.5, atypical lymphs, LFTs, renal
DRESS managementStop drug, Pred 1mg/kg, taper 6–12 weeks
AGEP featuresPustules, neutrophilia, 1–5 days, beta-lactams, benign
AHS = DRESS byAromatic AEDs (CZP, PHT, PB, OXC, LMG) — cross-reactivity HIGH
Non-aromatic AEDsLevetiracetam, Topiramate, Gabapentin, Lacosamide, Zonisamide, Pregabalin
Torsades mechanismhERG (IKr) blockade
Torsades acute RxIV MgSO4 2g bolus
Hy's LawALT>3xULN + Bil>2xULN = 10–50% mortality
HLA-B*15:02Carbamazepine/phenytoin/oxcarbazepine SJS/TEN (Asians)
HLA-B*58:01Allopurinol SCAR (Asians, CKD)
HLA-B*57:01Abacavir HSS (universal screen)
HLA-A*31:01Carbamazepine DRESS (Europeans/Japanese)
HLA-DRB1*07:01Flucloxacillin DILI (Class II)
HLA-B*13:01Dapsone HSS

15. 15. ✅ Answer Keys

1. MCQs

  1. B — Type B = idiosyncratic, unpredictable, high mortality
  2. D — Type IV = T-cell mediated
  3. B — SJS/TEN overlap = 10–30% BSA
  4. C — Allopurinol 3wks + fever + rash + eosinophilia + hepatitis = DRESS
  5. C — SCORTEN uses BUN not creatinine
  6. B — AGEP = pustules + neutrophilia
  7. B — Aromatic AEDs high cross-reactivity
  8. C — hERG (IKr) blockade
  9. B — IV MgSO₄ 2g bolus (first-line)
  10. B — Hy's Law = high mortality/transplant
  11. D — Flucloxacillin = Class II HLA-DRB107:01/DQA102:01
  12. B — Abacavir = B*57:01; stop permanently, never rechallenge
  13. B — Valproate = mitochondrial toxicity (beta-oxidation inhibition)
  14. B — CZP SJS/TEN = B*15:02
  15. A — DRESS = eosinophilia; AGEP = neutrophilia

File: /mnt/tb/Medicine/Clinical Therapeutics and Good Prescribing/ADRs/Type B (Bizarre).md | Status: draft → upgrade to full-fcps-mrcp-note after review

▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 2.10 Drug Interactions 12
▸ 2.10.01 Absorption Interactions

Status: draft | Chapter: 2 — Clinical Therapeutics and Good Prescribing | Heading: Drug Interactions → Pharmacokinetic Interactions | Exam Priority: ⭐⭐ HIGH (Common clinical scenarios, easy marks)

1. 1. 🎯 Learning Objectives

  • [ ] Identify mechanisms of absorption interactions: pH, chelation, gastric emptying/motility, transporters, binding resins
  • [ ] Apply spacing rules for common combinations
  • [ ] Recognise clinical significance — when to separate, when to avoid
  • [ ] Execute counselling points for patients

2. 2. 📊 Mechanisms of Absorption Interactions

MechanismDescriptionExamples
pH-Dependent SolubilityGastric pH alters dissolution/ionisation of weak acids/basesPPI/H2RA + Ketoconazole/Itraconazole/Atazanavir/Ledipasvir (↓ absorption)
Chelation / ComplexationMultivalent cations (Al³⁺, Mg²⁺, Ca²⁺, Fe²⁺/³⁺, Zn²⁺) bind drugs → insoluble complexesFluoroquinolones, Tetracyclines, Bisphosphonates, Thyroid hormones + Antacids, Iron, Calcium, Sucralfate, Multivitamins
Gastric Emptying / MotilityDrugs altering GI transit affect absorption of othersMetoclopramide/Domperidone (↑ emptying) + Paracetamol (↑ absorption); Anticholinergics/Opioids (↓ emptying) + Levodopa (↓ absorption)
Transporter-MediatedInflux (OATP, OCT, PEPT1) / Efflux (P-gp, BCRP, MRP2) at enterocyteP-gp inhibitors (Clarithromycin, Verapamil) + Digoxin (↑ absorption); OATP inhibitors (Rifampicin, Ciclosporin) + Statins (↑ absorption)
Binding Resins / AdsorbentsNon-specific binding in lumenCholestyramine/Colestipol/Colesevelam + Warfarin, Digoxin, Thyroid hormones, Thiazides, Statins, Fat-soluble vitamins
Food EffectsHigh-fat / high-calcium / grapefruit juiceGrapefruit juice (CYP3A4/P-gp inhibition) + Felodipine/Simvastatin/Ciclosporin; High-fat meal + Griseofulvin (↑): Calcium + Tetracyclines/Bisphosphonates (↓)

3. 3. ⚡ High-Yield Clinical Combinations

1. 1. Acid-Reducing Agents + pH-Dependent Drugs

Acid ReducerAffected DrugEffectManagement
PPI (Omeprazole, Esomeprazole, Pantoprazole)Ketoconazole, Itraconazole, Posaconazole↓ Absorption (need acidic pH)Avoid if possible; if essential: give antifungal with acidic drink (cola) 2h before PPI
PPIAtazanavir, Ledipasvir, Rilpivirine↓ AbsorptionContraindicated (atazanavir) / Avoid (ledipasvir) / Separate (rilpivirine: PPI 12h before)
PPIErlotinib, Gefitinib, Dasatinib, Nilotinib↓ AbsorptionAvoid PPI; use H2RA (separate 10h) or antacid (separate 2h)
H2RA (Ranitidine, Famotidine)Ketoconazole, Itraconazole↓ Absorption (less than PPI)Separate by ≥2h; give antifungal with acidic drink
Antacids (Al/Mg hydroxide)Ketoconazole, Itraconazole↓ AbsorptionSeparate by ≥2h

2. 2. Chelation — Must Separate

DrugChelating AgentSeparationClinical Pearl
Fluoroquinolones (Ciprofloxacin, Levofloxacin, Moxifloxacin, Ofloxacin)Antacids (Al/Mg/Ca), Iron, Zinc, Calcium, Sucralfate, Multivitamins, Dairy2h before or 4–6h afterCiprofloxacin + Ca²⁺ = ↓ 90% absorption
Tetracyclines (Doxycycline, Minocycline, Tetracycline)Antacids, Iron, Calcium, Magnesium, Dairy, Multivitamins2h before or 4h afterDoxycycline less affected (can take with food if GI upset)
Bisphosphonates (Alendronate, Risedronate, Ibandronate)Calcium, Iron, Magnesium, Antacids, Food, Beverages (except water)Take 30–60 min BEFORE food/drinks/meds with plain water only; remain uprightAbsolute requirement for absorption
Thyroid Hormones (Levothyroxine)Calcium, Iron, Sucralfate, Cholestyramine, Colestipol, PPIs, Soy, Walnuts, FibreTake 30–60 min BEFORE breakfast; separate other meds by ≥4hLifelong separation
DigoxinCholestyramine, Colestipol, Colesevelam, Sucralfate, Antacids (Al/Mg), Kaolin, PectinSeparate by ≥2hDigoxin narrow TI
WarfarinCholestyramine, Colestipol, Colesevelam, OrlistatSeparate by ≥2hINR monitoring

3. 3. Motility / Gastric Emptying

Drug Affecting MotilityAffected DrugEffectSignificance
Metoclopramide / Domperidone (↑ emptying)Paracetamol↑ Rate & extent of absorptionEarlier analgesia
Metoclopramide / DomperidoneLevodopa↑ Absorption (paradoxical — metoclopramide D2 antagonist worsens Parkinson's)AVOID metoclopramide in Parkinson's
Anticholinergics / Opioids / L-Dopa (↓ emptying)Paracetamol↓ Rate of absorptionDelayed analgesia
Anticholinergics / OpioidsLevodopa↓ AbsorptionWorsened motor control
Erythromycin (motilin agonist, ↑ emptying)Paracetamol↑ AbsorptionMinor

4. 4. Transporter Interactions (Intestinal)

TransporterInhibitor (↑ Absorption of Substrate)Inducer (↓ Absorption)Clinically Relevant Substrates
P-gp (ABCB1)Clarithromycin, Verapamil, Quinidine, Ciclosporin, Amiodarone, Dronedarone, RitonavirRifampicin, St John's Wort, Carbamazepine, PhenytoinDigoxin, Dabigatran, Rivaroxaban, Apixaban, Tacrolimus, Ciclosporin, Fexofenadine
OATP1A2/2B1Rifampicin (single dose), Ciclosporin, Gemfibrozil, RitonavirRifampicin (chronic)Statins (Rosuvastatin, Atorvastatin), Fexofenadine, Methotrexate
OCT1Verapamil, Quinidine, Cimetidine, Propranolol—Metformin, Imatinib
PEPT1——Beta-lactams (cephalexin), ACE inhibitors, Di/tripeptides

5. 5. Binding Resins (Cholestyramine, Colestipol, Colesevelam)

Affected DrugSeparationNotes
Warfarin≥2hINR monitoring
Digoxin≥2hNarrow TI
Thyroid Hormones≥4hCritical
Thiazide Diuretics≥1h
Statins≥1h
Fat-Soluble Vitamins (A, D, E, K)≥2hGive vitamins at bedtime
Folic Acid≥2h

4. 4. 📋 Patient Counselling Quick Reference

DrugKey Counselling Point
Bisphosphonates"Take first thing in morning with full glass of plain water, wait 30–60 min before ANY food, drink, or other meds, stay upright"
Levothyroxine"Take on empty stomach 30–60 min before breakfast; separate calcium, iron, antacids, PPIs by 4h"
Fluoroquinolones"Avoid dairy, antacids, iron, zinc, multivitamins 2h before or 4–6h after"
Tetracyclines"Avoid dairy, antacids, iron, calcium 2h before or 4h after"; doxycycline can take with food if nausea
Itraconazole / Ketoconazole"Take with food and acidic drink (cola); avoid PPIs/H2RAs/antacids or separate 2h"
Digoxin"Separate from cholestyramine, antacids, kaolin, pectin by 2h"
Warfarin"Separate from cholestyramine, orlistat by 2h; consistent vitamin K intake"
Statins"Separate from cholestyramine by ≥1h; avoid grapefruit juice" (esp. simvastatin, lovastatin, atorvastatin)

5. 5. 🎯 FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

CombinationEffectAction
PPI + Ketoconazole/Itraconazole↓ AbsorptionAvoid / give with cola 2h before PPI
PPI + Atazanavir↓ AbsorptionContraindicated
Antacids + Ciprofloxacin↓ 90% absorptionSeparate 2h before / 4–6h after
Calcium + Levothyroxine↓ AbsorptionLevothyroxine 30–60 min before breakfast; Ca 4h later
Cholestyramine + Warfarin/Digoxin/Thyroxine↓ AbsorptionSeparate ≥2h (thyroxine ≥4h)
Metoclopramide + Levodopa↑ Absorption but worsens Parkinson's (D2 antagonist)AVOID metoclopramide in Parkinson's
Grapefruit juice + Felodipine/Simvastatin/Ciclosporin↑ Bioavailability (CYP3A4/P-gp inhibition)Avoid grapefruit juice
Rifampicin (chronic) + OATP substrates (Statins)↓ Absorption (induction)Dose adjust / monitor

6. 6. ❓ Viva Questions (8)

QAnswer
1. Why should PPIs be avoided with ketoconazole/itraconazole?Azoles require acidic gastric pH for dissolution/absorption; PPIs raise pH → ↓ bioavailability
2. Ciprofloxacin + antacid containing calcium — separation required?2h before or 4–6h after — chelation forms insoluble complex
3. Levothyroxine + calcium carbonate — how to separate?Levothyroxine 30–60 min before breakfast; calcium ≥4h later
4. Bisphosphonate administration rules?First thing AM, full glass plain water, wait 30–60 min before food/drink/meds, remain upright
5. Metoclopramide in Parkinson's patient — why avoid?D2 antagonist → worsens motor symptoms; domperidone preferred (peripheral D2 only)
6. Grapefruit juice interaction — mechanism, affected drugs?Intestinal CYP3A4 + P-gp inhibition → ↑ bioavailability of felodipine, simvastatin, ciclosporin, tacrolimus, rivaroxaban
5. Cholestyramine + warfarin — separation?≥2h (warfarin narrow TI, INR monitoring)
6. Rifampicin + oral contraceptive / statin — effect on absorption?Chronic rifampicin induces OATP/P-gp → ↓ absorption of statins, OCP; acute rifampicin inhibits

7. 7. 🤯 Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
Doxycycline vs other tetracyclines + foodDoxycycline can be taken with food (less chelation); other tetracyclines must be on empty stomach
Acute vs chronic rifampicin on transportersAcute: inhibits OATP/P-gp → ↑ absorption; Chronic: induces → ↓ absorption
PPI + atazanavirContraindicated — cannot overcome with separation; use alternative ARV
Bisphosphonate + water onlyPlain water only — coffee, juice, mineral water all ↓ absorption
Levothyroxine + PPIPPI ↓ gastric acidity → ↓ levothyroxine dissolution; separate by ≥4h

Mnemonics:

  • "BISPHOSPHONATE RULES" = Before breakfast, Immediately on waking, Stay upright, Plain water only, Half-hour wait, Other meds later, Separate calcium/iron, PPIs separate, H2RAs separate, Antacids separate, Take weekly/monthly, Every dose same way
  • "LEVO RULES" = Levothyroxine: Empty stomach, Very early (30–60 min before food), Only water; separate Calcium, Iron, PPI, Antacids, Sucralfate
  • "QUINOLONE CHELATION" = Quinolones + Calcium/Iron/Mg/Al/Zn = Separate 2h before / 4–6h after
  • "GRAPEFRUIT = CYP3A4/P-gp INHIBITOR" = affects Felodipine, Simvastatin, Ciclosporin, Tacrolimus, Rivaroxaban

8. 8. 🧠 Mind Map (Mermaid)

Absorption Interactions
pH-Dependent
PPI/H2RA + Azoles (Ketoconazole, Itraconazole) → ↓ absorption
PPI + Atazanavir/Ledipasvir → ↓ absorption (AVOID Atazanavir)
PPI + TKIs (Erlotinib, Dasatinib) → ↓ absorption
Chelation
Fluoroquinolones + Ca/Mg/Al/Fe/Zn → Separate 2h/4-6h
Tetracyclines + Ca/Mg/Fe → Separate 2h/4h (Doxycycline with food OK)
Bisphosphonates + Food/Ca/Fe → 30-60min BEFORE, water only, upright
Levothyroxine + Ca/Fe/Sucralfate/PPI → 30-60min BEFORE breakfast, separate 4h
Digoxin/Warfarin + Cholestyramine → Separate 2h
Motility
Metoclopramide/Domperidone → ↑ Paracetamol absorption
Metoclopramide + Levodopa → AVOID (D2 antagonist worsens Parkinson's)
Anticholinergics/Opioids → ↓ Levodopa/Paracetamol absorption
Transporters (Intestinal)
P-gp: Clarithro/Verapamil/Quinidine inhibit → ↑ Digoxin/Dabigatran/Rivaroxaban
OATP: Rifampicin chronic induces → ↓ Statins/Fexofenadine
OCT1: Verapamil/Cimetidine inhibit → ↑ Metformin
Binding Resins
Cholestyramine/Colestipol/Colesevelam → Bind Warfarin, Digoxin, Thyroxine, Thiazides, Statins, Vit A/D/E/K
Separate ≥2h (Thyroxine ≥4h)
Food
Grapefruit juice → CYP3A4/P-gp inhibition → ↑ Felodipine/Simvastatin/Ciclosporin
High-fat → ↑ Griseofulvin
Calcium → ↓ Tetracyclines/Bisphosphonates

9. 9. 📅 Spaced Repetition Tracker

ReviewDateScoreNext
11d
23d

42w
51m
63m

10. 10. 🧪 Self-Test Scorecard

SectionMaxScore
pH-dependent table8
Chelation table12
Motility effects6
Transporter table8
Binding resins6
Counselling points8
Viva answers8
Total56

Target: ≥45/56 (80%)

11. 11. 📝 Exam Answer Modes

1. Short Question (5 marks): "Bisphosphonate administration rules"

  • First thing in morning on waking
  • Full glass plain water only
  • Remain upright (sit/stand) for 30–60 min
  • No food, drink, or other medications for 30–60 min
  • Separate calcium, iron, antacids, PPIs by ≥30–60 min

2. Viva (1 min): "Patient on ciprofloxacin 500mg BD prescribed calcium carbonate for osteoporosis. Counselling?"

  • Chelation → ↓ ciprofloxacin absorption up to 90%
  • Separate by 2h before or 4–6h after calcium dose
  • Advise take ciprofloxacin 2h before breakfast, calcium with dinner

3. Ward Round (30 sec): "Patient on levothyroxine 100mcg, started omeprazole 20mg for dyspepsia. TSH rising. Why?"

  • PPI ↓ gastric acidity → ↓ levothyroxine dissolution/absorption
  • Separate by ≥4h (levothyroxine 30–60 min before breakfast, omeprazole 30 min before dinner)
  • Monitor TSH in 6–8 weeks

4. Last-Night Revision (1-liners):

  • PPI + ketoconazole/itraconazole = ↓ absorption (need acid) → give with cola, separate from PPI
  • PPI + atazanavir = CONTRAINDICATED
  • Ciprofloxacin + calcium/antacids = separate 2h/4-6h
  • Doxycycline = can take with food
  • Bisphosphonate = plain water, upright, 30-60min wait
  • Levothyroxine = empty stomach 30-60min before breakfast, separate Ca/Fe/PPI/antacids 4h
  • Cholestyramine = binds warfarin/digoxin/thyroxine → separate 2h (thyroxine 4h)
  • Metoclopramide + levodopa = AVOID (D2 antagonist)
  • Grapefruit = CYP3A4/P-gp inhibition → felodipine, simvastatin, ciclosporin

12. 12. 📚 Summary Card

ABSORPTION INTERACTION TRIAD:
1. CHELATION = Fluoroquinolones/Tetracyclines/Bisphosphonates/Levothyroxine + Cations → SEPARATE
2. pH = PPI/H2RA + Azoles/Atazanavir/TKIs → AVOID / SEPARATE / ACIDIC DRINK
3. TRANSPORTERS = P-gp/OATP inhibitors/inducers → DIGOXIN/DOAC/STATIN dose adjust

13. 13. ❓ MCQs (12)

  1. Ketoconazole absorption requires:

A. Alkaline pH

B. Acidic pH ✓

C. Neutral pH

D. Food only

E. No specific pH

  1. Ciprofloxacin + calcium carbonate — recommended separation:

A. Take together

B. 2h before or 4–6h after ✓

C. 1h before or 2h after

D. 30 min before

E. No separation needed

  1. Levothyroxine administration — correct instruction:

A. With breakfast

B. 30–60 min before breakfast, empty stomach, water only ✓

C. At bedtime

D. With calcium supplement

E. With PPI

  1. Bisphosphonate (alendronate) — correct administration:

A. With breakfast and orange juice

B. First thing AM, plain water, upright 30–60 min, no food/meds ✓

C. At bedtime with milk

C. With calcium supplement

E. Any time with food

  1. PPI + Atazanavir — correct statement:

A. Safe with 2h separation

B. Contraindicated ✓

C. Increase atazanavir dose

D. Use H2RA instead

E. No interaction

  1. Grapefruit juice inhibits intestinal:

A. CYP2C9

B. CYP3A4 and P-gp ✓

C. CYP2D6

D. CYP1A2

E. UGT1A1

  1. Cholestyramine binds which drug requiring ≥4h separation?

A. Digoxin

B. Warfarin

C. Levothyroxine ✓

D. Furosemide

E. Atorvastatin

  1. Metoclopramide in Parkinson's disease — why avoid?

A. Causes hypotension

B. D2 antagonist → worsens motor symptoms ✓

C. Increases levodopa toxicity

D. Causes seizures

E. No interaction

  1. Rifampicin chronic effect on OATP substrates (statins):

A. ↑ Absorption

B. ↓ Absorption (induction) ✓

C. No effect

D. Unpredictable

E. Only acute effect

  1. Doxycycline vs Tetracycline — food interaction difference:

A. Both must be fasting

B. Doxycycline can be taken with food; Tetracycline cannot ✓

C. Tetracycline can be taken with food; Doxycycline cannot

D. Both can be taken with food

E. Neither affected by food

  1. Acute rifampicin effect on transporters:

A. Induces P-gp

B. Inhibits OATP/P-gp → ↑ absorption ✓

C. No effect

D. Induces CYP3A4 only

E. Inhibits CYP2C9

  1. Warfarin + cholestyramine separation:

A. No separation needed

B. ≥2h ✓

C. ≥4h

D. ≥6h

E. 30 min

14. 14. 🃏 Flashcards (Anki-ready)

FrontBack
Ketoconazole pH requirementAcidic gastric pH for absorption
PPI + Azoles↓ Absorption → give with cola, separate from PPI
PPI + AtazanavirCONTRAINDICATED
Ciprofloxacin + Ca/Mg/Al/Fe/ZnSeparate 2h before / 4–6h after
Tetracycline + Ca/Mg/FeSeparate 2h before / 4h after (Doxycycline with food OK)
Bisphosphonate rulesAM, plain water, upright 30-60min, no food/meds
Levothyroxine rules30-60min before breakfast, separate Ca/Fe/PPI/antacid 4h
Cholestyramine bindsWarfarin, Digoxin, Thyroxine, Thiazides, Statins, Vit A/D/E/K
Cholestyramine + ThyroxineSeparate ≥4h
Metoclopramide + Parkinson'sAVOID (D2 antagonist)
Grapefruit juiceIntestinal CYP3A4 + P-gp inhibition
Grapefruit affectsFelodipine, Simvastatin, Ciclosporin, Tacrolimus, Rivaroxaban
Rifampicin acuteInhibits OATP/P-gp → ↑ absorption
Rifampicin chronicInduces OATP/P-gp → ↓ absorption

15. 15. ✅ Answer Keys

1. MCQs

  1. B — Acidic pH
  2. B — 2h before or 4–6h after
  3. B — 30–60 min before breakfast, empty stomach
  4. B — First thing AM, plain water, upright, wait
  5. B — Contraindicated
  6. B — CYP3A4 and P-gp
  7. C — Levothyroxine (≥4h)
  8. B — D2 antagonist worsens motor symptoms
  9. B — Chronic rifampicin induces → ↓ statin absorption
  10. B — Doxycycline with food OK
  11. B — Acute rifampicin inhibits OATP/P-gp
  12. B — ≥2h separation

File: /mnt/tb/Medicine/Clinical Therapeutics and Good Prescribing/Drug Interactions/Absorption interactions.md | Status: draft → upgrade after review

▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 2.10.02 Anti Epileptic Interactions

Status: draft | Chapter: 2 — Clinical Therapeutics and Good Prescribing | Heading: Drug Interactions → High-Risk Combination Profiles | Exam Priority: ⭐⭐⭐ HIGH (Neurology, enzyme induction/inhibition, narrow TI, polypharmacy)

1. 1. 🎯 Learning Objectives

  • [ ] Classify AEDs by enzyme induction/inhibition potential
  • [ ] Identify critical interactions: AED–AED, AED–Non-AED (OCP, Warfarin, DOACs, Steroids, ARVs, TB drugs, Antidepressants)
  • [ ] Apply monitoring strategies (levels, clinical, labs)
  • [ ] Manage key scenarios: Pregnancy, Renal/Hepatic impairment, Status epilepticus
  • [ ] Recognise HLA screening indications (Carbamazepine, Phenytoin)

2. 2. 📊 AED Classification by Enzyme Effect

CategoryDrugsEnzyme EffectKey Interactions
Strong InducersCarbamazepine (CBZ), Phenytoin (PHT), Phenobarbital (PB), Primidone (PRM)Induce CYP3A4, 2C9, 2C19, 1A2, UGT, P-gp↓ Levels of: OCP, Warfarin, DOACs, Steroids, ARVs, TB drugs, Antidepressants, CCBs, Statins, Immunosuppressants, Theophylline
Moderate InducersOxcarbazepine (OXC), Eslicarbazepine (ESL), Topiramate (TPM) (>200mg), Rufinamide, PerampanelInduce CYP3A4, UGT (weaker)Similar but less potent; OXC/ESL induce CYP3A4; TPM dose-dependent
Neutral / MinimalLevetiracetam (LEV), Lamotrigine (LTG), Lacosamide (LCM), Gabapentin (GBP), Pregabalin (PGB), Vigabatrin (VGB), Brivaracetam (BRV), Zonisamide (ZNS), Ethosuximide (ESM), Valproate (VPA) — see belowNo significant CYP inductionPreferred in polypharmacy; LTG levels ↓ by inducers; VPA inhibits some CYP/UGT

| Inhibitors | Valproate (VPA), Stiripentol (STP), Rufinamide (weak), Cannabidiol (CBD) | VPA: Inhibits UGT, CYP2C9, CYP2C19, β-oxidation, epoxide hydrolase

STP: Potent CYP3A4, 2C19, UGT inhibitor | ↑ Levels of: LTG (2x), CBZ-epoxide, PHT, PB, ZNS, Topiramate, Cannabidiol, Rufinamide |

Valproate = SPECIAL — Inhibits LTG glucuronidation (↑ LTG 2x); Inhibits CBZ epoxide hydrolase (↑ CBZ-epoxide → toxicity); Displaces PHT from albumin (↑ free PHT); Inhibits β-oxidation (hyperammonaemia risk)

3. 3. ⚡ Critical Interaction Scenarios

1. 1. Enzyme-Inducing AEDs (CBZ, PHT, PB) + Non-AED Drugs

Drug ClassEffectClinical ConsequenceManagement
Combined OCP↓ Ethinylestradiol 40–60%Contraceptive failureAdd barrier method; Use IUD/Implant (levonorgestrel IUS preferred); Avoid progestin-only pill with CBZ/PHT/PB
Progestin-only Pill / Implant↓ ProgestinFailure riskAvoid implant with inducers; Use IUS or DMPA injection (less affected)
Warfarin↓ Warfarin levelsSubtherapeutic INR → ThrombosisIncrease Warfarin dose; Monitor INR 2x/week after AED change
DOACs (Apixaban, Rivaroxaban, Dabigatran, Edoxaban)↓ DOAC levels (CYP3A4/P-gp induction)Thrombosis riskAVOID DOACs with strong inducers; Use Warfarin (with INR monitoring) or UFH/LMWH
Corticosteroids (Prednisolone, Dexamethasone, Budesonide)↓ Steroid levelsTreatment failure (asthma, COPD, autoimmune)Increase steroid dose; Consider non-inducing AED
Antiretrovirals (PIs, INSTIs, NNRTIs)↓ ARV levelsVirological failureAVOID strong inducers with ARVs; Use non-inducing AEDs (LEV, LTG, LCM, VPA)
Anti-TB (Rifampicin, Rifabutin)↓ Rifamycin levelsTB treatment failureRifampicin induces AEDs too — bidirectional; Use non-inducing AEDs
Calcineurin Inhibitors (Tacrolimus, Ciclosporin)↓ Levels → RejectionTransplant rejectionIncrease CNI dose; Monitor trough levels 2x/week
Antidepressants (SSRIs, SNRIs, TCAs)↓ LevelsDepression relapseMonitor; ↑ dose if needed
CCBs (Verapamil, Diltiazem), Statins↓ LevelsLoss of BP/lipid controlMonitor; ↑ dose

2. 2. Valproate (VPA) — Special Inhibitor Profile

InteractionMechanismClinical EffectManagement
Lamotrigine (LTG)Inhibits UGT1A4 → ↓ LTG glucuronidationLTG levels ↑ 2x → Rash/SJS riskLTG dose ↓ 50%; Slow titration (25mg q2wks if with VPA)
Carbamazepine (CBZ)Inhibits epoxide hydrolase → ↑ CBZ-epoxideCBZ-epoxide ↑ → Neurotoxicity (tremor, ataxia, diplopia)Monitor CBZ + CBZ-epoxide levels; ↓ CBZ dose
Phenytoin (PHT)Displaces PHT from albumin + inhibits CYP2C9Free PHT ↑↑ → Toxicity (nystagmus, ataxia) at normal total levelMonitor FREE PHT level; ↓ PHT dose
Phenobarbital (PB)Inhibits PB metabolismPB levels ↑Monitor PB level; ↓ PB dose
Zonisamide (ZNS)Inhibits ZNS metabolismZNS levels ↑Monitor; ↓ ZNS dose
Topiramate (TPM)Inhibits TPM metabolism (weak)TPM levels ↑ slightlyMonitor
Cannabidiol (CBD)Inhibits CYP2C19, CYP3A4CBD levels↑, Clobazam metabolite ↑Monitor; ↓ Clobazam dose
RufinamideInhibits Rufinamide metabolismRufinamide levels ↑Monitor

3. 3. AED–AED Interactions (Key Pairs)

AED PairInteractionClinical EffectManagement
CBZ + LTGCBZ induces LTG glucuronidationLTG levels ↓ 40–50%↑ LTG dose; monitor level
CBZ + PHTMutual induction/protein binding displacementUnpredictable levelsMonitor both FREE levels
VPA + LTGVPA inhibits LTG glucuronidationLTG ↑ 2x → SJS riskLTG dose 50%; slow titration
VPA + PHTVPA displaces PHT + inhibits CYP2C9Free PHT ↑↑Monitor FREE PHT; ↓ PHT dose
VPA + CBZVPA inhibits CBZ epoxide hydrolaseCBZ-epoxide ↑ → NeurotoxicityMonitor CBZ-epoxide; ↓ CBZ dose
VPA + PBVPA inhibits PB metabolismPB levels ↑Monitor PB level
PHT + LTGPHT induces LTGLTG levels ↓↑ LTG dose
TPM + OCPsTPM (>200mg) induces CYP3A4↓ EthinylestradiolAdd barrier method
OXC/ESL + OCPOXC/ESL induce CYP3A4↓ EthinylestradiolAdd barrier method

4. 4. Other Key Non-AED Interactions

DrugInteracting AEDsEffectManagement
Antidepressants (SSRIs, TCAs, Bupropion, MAOIs)CBZ, PHT, PB (inducers)↓ Antidepressant levelsMonitor; ↑ dose; consider non-inducing AED
Antipsychotics (Clozapine, Olanzapine, Quetiapine, Risperidone)CBZ, PHT, PB↓ Levels → RelapseMonitor; ↑ dose; avoid clozapine with CBZ (↑ agranulocytosis risk)
Benzodiazepines (Clonazepam, Clobazam, Midazolam, Diazepam)CBZ, PHT, PB (inducers)↓ BZD levelsClobazam + VPA → ↑ N-desmethylclobazam (active)
Methadone / BuprenorphineCBZ, PHT, PB, EFV, Rifampicin↓ Levels → Withdrawal↑ Methadone dose; Monitor
TheophyllineCBZ, PHT, PB↓ Theophylline levelsMonitor level; ↑ dose
Ciclosporin / TacrolimusCBZ, PHT, PB↓ Levels → RejectionMonitor trough 2x/week; ↑ dose

4. 4. 🎯 FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

PearlDetails
Strong InducersCBZ, PHT, PB, PRM — Induce CYP3A4, 2C9, 2C19, 1A2, UGT, P-gp
Non-inducing AEDs (Preferred)LEV, LTG, LCM, GBP, PGB, VGB, BRV, ZNS, ESM
ValproateInhibits: LTG glucuronidation (↑ 2x), CBZ epoxide hydrolase (↑ epoxide), PHT displacement (↑ free PHT)
OCP + Inducers↓ Ethinylestradiol 40–60% → Barrier method; IUD preferred
Warfarin + Inducers↓ Warfarin → ↑ Warfarin dose, INR 2x/week
DOACs + InducersAVOID DOACs → Use Warfarin or LMWH
Steroids + Inducers↓ Steroid levels → ↑ Steroid dose
ARVs + InducersAVOID — Use non-inducing AEDs (LEV, LTG, LCM, VPA)
LTG + VPALTG ↑ 2x → LTG dose 50%, slow titration
LTG + CBZLTG ↓ 40–50% → ↑ LTG dose
HLA-B*15:02Screen before CBZ/OXC/ESL in Asian ancestry (SJS/TEN risk)
HLA-A*31:01Screen before CBZ in European/Japanese (DRESS/SJS risk)

5. 5. ❓ Viva Questions (10)

QAnswer
1. Strong enzyme-inducing AEDs — list 4?Carbamazepine, Phenytoin, Phenobarbital, Primidone
2. Non-inducing AEDs (preferred in polypharmacy) — list 5?Levetiracetam, Lamotrigine, Lacosamide, Gabapentin, Pregabalin, Vigabatrin, Brivaracetam, Zonisamide, Ethosuximide
3. Valproate + Lamotrigine — interaction & management?VPA inhibits LTG glucuronidation → LTG levels ↑ 2x → LTG dose 50%, slow titration (25mg q2wks)
4. Valproate + Phenytoin — interaction & monitoring?VPA displaces PHT from albumin + inhibits CYP2C9 → Free PHT ↑↑ → Monitor FREE PHT level (total unreliable); ↓ PHT dose
5. Valproate + Carbamazepine — toxic metabolite?CBZ-epoxide (VPA inhibits epoxide hydrolase) → Neurotoxicity (tremor, ataxia, diplopia)
6. Enzyme-inducing AED + Combined OCP — failure risk?↓ Ethinylestradiol 40–60% → Contraceptive failure → Add barrier method; IUD preferred
7. Enzyme-inducing AED + Warfarin — management?Inducers ↑ Warfarin metabolism → Subtherapeutic INR → Increase Warfarin dose; Monitor INR 2x/week
8. Enzyme-inducing AED + DOACs — recommendation?AVOID DOACs → Use Warfarin (with INR monitoring) or LMWH/UFH
9. HLA screening before Carbamazepine — which alleles, which populations?HLA-B15:02 (Asian ancestry — SJS/TEN); HLA-A31:01 (European/Japanese — DRESS/SJS)
10. Lamotrigine + Carbamazepine — LTG levels?CBZ induces LTG glucuronidation → LTG levels ↓ 40–50% → Increase LTG dose

6. 6. 🤯 Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
Inducers vs InhibitorsInducers (CBZ, PHT, PB) = ↓ levels of other drugs; Inhibitors (VPA, STP) = ↑ levels of specific drugs
Valproate = Inhibitor?Yes but complex — Inhibits UGT (↑ LTG), epoxide hydrolase (↑ CBZ-epoxide), displaces PHT (↑ free PHT)
LTG dose with VPA vs CBZVPA → LTG dose 50%; CBZ → LTG dose ↑
Free vs Total PhenytoinWith VPA or hypoalbuminaemia → Use FREE level (total underestimates effect)
DOACs + InducersAVOID — use Warfarin/LMWH instead
Progestin-only implant + InducersAvoid — failure risk; Use IUS or DMPA injection

Mnemonics:

  • "INDUCERS = CBZ, PHT, PB, PRIMIDONE" = Strong CYP3A4/2C9/2C19/1A2/UGT/P-gp inducers
  • "NON-INDUCERS = LEV, LTG, LCM, GBP, PGB, VGB, BRV, ZNS, ETHOSUXIMIDE" = Safe in polypharmacy
  • "VALPROATE = INHIBITOR" = ↑ LTG 2x (dose 50%); ↑ CBZ-epoxide (neurotoxicity); ↑ Free PHT (free level!)
  • "LTG + VPA = HALF DOSE"; "LTG + CBZ = DOUBLE DOSE"
  • "FREE PHENYTOIN" = Hypoalbuminaemia, VPA, Renal failure, Elderly
  • "INDUCERS + OCP = BARRIER METHOD" = 40-60% ↓ ethinylestradiol
  • "INDUCERS + WARFARIN = ↑ DOSE, INR 2x/WEEK"
  • "INDUCERS + DOAC = AVOID"
  • "HLA-B*15:02 = ASIAN + CBZ/OXC/ESL"
  • "HLA-A*31:01 = EUROPEAN/JAPANESE + CBZ"

7. 7. 🧠 Mind Map (Mermaid)

Anti-Epileptic Interactions
Strong Inducers
Carbamazepine, Phenytoin, Phenobarbital, Primidone
Induce: CYP3A4, 2C9, 2C19, 1A2, UGT, P-gp
↓ OCP, Warfarin, DOACs, Steroids, ARVs, TB, Immunosuppressants
Non-Inducers (Preferred)
Levetiracetam, Lamotrigine, Lacosamide, Gabapentin
Pregabalin, Vigabatrin, Brivaracetam, Zonisamide, Ethosuximide
Valproate (Inhibitor)
↑ Lamotrigine 2x (UGT1A4) → LTG dose 50%
↑ CBZ-epoxide (epoxide hydrolase) → Neurotoxicity
↑ Free Phenytoin (displacement + CYP2C9 inhibition) → Free PHT level
↑ Phenobarbital, Zonisamide
AED-AED Key Pairs
CBZ + LTG → LTG ↓ 40-50% (↑ LTG dose)
VPA + LTG → LTG ↑ 2x (LTG dose 50%)
VPA + PHT → Free PHT ↑↑ (FREE level)
VPA + CBZ → CBZ-epoxide ↑ (Neurotoxicity)
PHT + LTG → LTG ↓ (↑ LTG dose)
Non-AED Interactions
OCP + Inducers → Barrier method / IUD
Warfarin + Inducers → ↑ Warfarin dose, INR 2x/week
DOACs + Inducers → AVOID (use Warfarin/LMWH)
Steroids + Inducers → ↑ Steroid dose
ARVs + Inducers → AVOID (use non-inducing AEDs)
CNIs + Inducers → Monitor trough 2x/week
HLA Screening
HLA-B*15:02 → Asian + CBZ/OXC/ESL (SJS/TEN)
HLA-A*31:01 → European/Japanese + CBZ (DRESS/SJS)

8. 8. 📅 Spaced Repetition Tracker

ReviewDateScoreNext
11d
23d

63m

9. 9. 🧪 Self-Test Scorecard

SectionMaxScore
Inducers vs Non-inducers8
Valproate interactions8
AED-AED pairs8
Non-AED interactions8
HLA screening4
Viva answers10
Total46

Target: ≥37/46 (80%)

10. 10. 📝 Exam Answer Modes

1. Short Question (5 marks): "Enzyme-inducing AEDs and their impact on non-AED drugs."

  • Strong inducers: CBZ, PHT, PB, Primidone — Induce CYP3A4, 2C9, 2C19, 1A2, UGT, P-gp
  • Effect on non-AEDs: ↓ Levels of OCP (↓ ethinylestradiol 40–60%), Warfarin, DOACs, Steroids, ARVs, TB drugs, CNIs, CCBs, Statins
  • Management: OCP → Barrier/IUD; Warfarin → ↑ dose, INR 2x/week; DOACs → AVOID (use Warfarin/LMWH); Steroids → ↑ dose; ARVs → Avoid inducers, use LEV/LTG/LCM/VPA

2. Viva (1 min): "Patient on Carbamazepine, started on Lamotrigine. 2 weeks later: diffuse rash, fever. Why? Management?"

  • CBZ induces LTG glucuronidation → LTG levels ↓ but rash occurs during titration
  • Rapid titration + CBZ induction → LTG levels fluctuating → Hypersensitivity risk
  • Management: STOP LTG immediately (SJS/TEN risk); Switch to non-aromatic AED (Levetiracetam, Lacosamide); Avoid rechallenge; Consider HLA-B*15:02 if Asian

3. Ward Round (30 sec): "Patient on Valproate 1000mg, started Phenytoin 300mg for breakthrough seizures. 1 week later: nystagmus, ataxia, confusion. Total PHT level 18 µg/mL (therapeutic). Why toxic?"

  • VPA displaces PHT from albumin + inhibits CYP2C9 → Free PHT ↑↑
  • Total PHT level MISLEADING (normal 10–20, but free ↑↑)
  • Check FREE PHT level — will be >2 µg/mL
  • ↓ Phenytoin dose; Monitor free level

4. Last-Night Revision (1-liners):

  • Inducers = CBZ, PHT, PB, Primidone (CYP3A4/2C9/2C19/1A2/UGT/P-gp)
  • Non-inducers = LEV, LTG, LCM, GBP, PGB, VGB, BRV, ZNS, ESM
  • VPA = inhibitor: ↑ LTG 2x (dose 50%), ↑ CBZ-epoxide, ↑ Free PHT
  • LTG + CBZ = ↑ LTG dose; LTG + VPA = LTG dose 50%
  • Free PHT when: VPA, hypoalbuminaemia, renal, elderly
  • Inducers + OCP = barrier/IUD; Inducers + Warfarin = ↑ dose, INR 2x/wk
  • Inducers + DOACs = AVOID
  • HLA-B15:02 = Asian + CBZ/OXC/ESL; HLA-A31:01 = Euro/Japanese + CBZ

11. 11. 📚 Summary Card

AED INTERACTIONS:
INDUCERS = CBZ, PHT, PB, Primidone → ↓ OCP, Warfarin, DOACs, Steroids, ARVs, CNIs
NON-INDUCERS = LEV, LTG, LCM, GBP, PGB, VGB, BRV, ZNS, ESM
VALPROATE = INHIBITOR → ↑ LTG 2x (½ dose), ↑ CBZ-epoxide, ↑ Free PHT (FREE level!)
LTG + CBZ = ↑ LTG dose; LTG + VPA = ½ LTG dose
FREE PHT = VPA, Hypoalbuminaemia, Renal, Elderly
INDUCERS + OCP = BARRIER/IUD; + WARFARIN = ↑ DOSE, INR 2x/wk; + DOAC = AVOID
HLA = B15:02 (Asian+CBZ), A31:01 (Euro/Jap+CBZ)

12. 12. ❓ MCQs (12)

  1. Strong enzyme-inducing AEDs:

A. Levetiracetam, Lamotrigine, Lacosamide

B. Carbamazepine, Phenytoin, Phenobarbital, Primidone ✓

C. Valproate, Gabapentin, Pregabalin

D. Topiramate, Zonisamide, Ethosuximide

E. Lacosamide, Brivaracetam, Vigabatrin

  1. Valproate + Lamotrigine — effect on LTG levels:

A. ↓ 50%

B. No change

C. ↑ 2x ✓

D. ↓ 2x

E. Unpredictable

  1. Valproate + Phenytoin — which level to monitor?

A. Total PHT

B. Free PHT ✓

C. VPA level

D. Albumin

E. CBZ-epoxide

  1. Enzyme-inducing AED + Combined OCP — ethinylestradiol change:

A. ↑ 50%

B. ↓ 40–60% ✓

C. No change

D. ↑ 2x

D. Unpredictable

  1. Enzyme-inducing AED + Warfarin — management:

A. ↓ Warfarin dose

B. ↑ Warfarin dose, INR 2x/week ✓

C. Switch to DOAC

D. No change

E. Stop Warfarin

  1. Enzyme-inducing AED + DOACs — recommendation:

A. Safe

B. AVOID (use Warfarin/LMWH) ✓

C. ↑ DOAC dose

D. Monitor anti-Xa only

E. Use edoxaban only

  1. Lamotrigine + Carbamazepine — LTG levels:

A. ↑ 2x

B. ↓ 40–50% ✓

C. No change

D. ↓ 2x

E. Unpredictable

  1. HLA-B*15:02 screening — population & drug:

A. European + Phenytoin

B. Asian + Carbamazepine/Oxcarbazepine/Eslicarbazepine ✓

C. African + Valproate

D. All populations + all AEDs

E. Japanese + Phenobarbital

  1. HLA-A*31:01 screening — population & drug:

A. Asian + Carbamazepine

B. European/Japanese + Carbamazepine ✓

C. All + Phenytoin

D. African + Lamotrigine

E. All + Valproate

  1. Valproate + Carbamazepine — toxic metabolite:

A. CBZ-diol

B. CBZ-epoxide ✓

C. CBZ-trans-diol

D. CBZ-imidazoline

E. VPA-glucuronide

  1. Non-inducing AEDs (preferred in polypharmacy):

A. CBZ, PHT, PB

B. LEV, LTG, LCM, GBP, PGB, VGB, BRV, ZNS, ESM ✓

C. TPM, OXC, ESL

D. VPA, STP, CBD

E. PRM, PHB, PHT

  1. Valproate inhibits epoxide hydrolase → ↑ CBZ-epoxide → clinical effect:

A. Hepatotoxicity

B. Neurotoxicity (tremor, ataxia, diplopia) ✓

C. Rash

D. Hyponatraemia

E. Thrombocytopenia

14. 14. ✅ Answer Keys

1. MCQs

  1. B — Carbamazepine, Phenytoin, Phenobarbital, Primidone

  3. B — Free PHT
  4. B — ↓ 40–60%
  5. B — ↑ Warfarin dose, INR 2x/week

  9. B — European/Japanese + Carbamazepine

File: /mnt/tb/Medicine/Clinical Therapeutics and Good Prescribing/Drug Interactions/Anti-epileptic interactions.md | Status: draft → upgrade after review

▼  Show infographic image click to expand
▸ 2.10.03 Antiretroviral Interactions

Status: draft | Chapter: 2 — Clinical Therapeutics and Good Prescribing | Heading: Drug Interactions → High-Risk Combination Profiles | Exam Priority: ⭐⭐⭐ HIGH (HIV medicine, polypharmacy, CYP3A4/UGT/P-gp complexity)

1. 1. 🎯 Learning Objectives

  • [ ] Classify ARV drug classes by interaction potential (PIs, NNRTIs, INSTIs, Boosted regimens)
  • [ ] Identify contraindicated combinations (CYP3A4/UGT/P-gp)
  • [ ] Apply boosting principles (Ritonavir/Cobicistat) and interaction management
  • [ ] Recognise key interactions: TB drugs, anticonvulsants, antifungals, statins, contraceptives, PPIs
  • [ ] Execute management: dose adjust, substitute, monitor

2. 2. 📊 ARV Classes & Interaction Profiles

ClassDrugsKey Enzymes/TransportersInteraction Risk
Protease Inhibitors (PIs)Atazanavir (ATV), Darunavir (DRV), Lopinavir (LPV), Ritonavir (RTV)CYP3A4 SUBSTRATE + STRONG INHIBITOR (esp. RTV); P-gp substrate + inhibitorHIGHEST — Inhibit CYP3A4, CYP2D6, P-gp, UGT1A1; ↑ levels of co-meds

| Pharmacokinetic Boosters | Ritonavir (RTV) 100mg, Cobicistat (COBI) 150mg | RTV: CYP3A4 STRONG INHIBITOR + INDUCER (CYP1A2, 2B6, 2C9, 2C19, UGT)

COBI: CYP3A4 STRONG INHIBITOR (no induction) | CRITICAL — Boost PI/INSTI levels; inhibit CYP3A4/P-gp of co-meds |

| NNRTIs | Efavirenz (EFV), Nevirapine (NVP), Rilpivirine (RPV), Doravirine (DOR), Etravirine (ETR) | EFV/NVP: CYP3A4 INDUCER + INHIBITOR (mixed)

RPV/DOR: CYP3A4 SUBSTRATE (no induction)

ETR: CYP3A4 INHIBITOR, CYP2B6/2C9/2C19 INDUCER | HIGH — EFV/NVP induce CYP3A4 (↓ co-med levels); RPV/DOR need acid |

| INSTIs | Raltegravir (RAL), Dolutegravir (DTG), Bictegravir (BIC), Cabotegravir (CAB) | RAL: UGT1A1 substrate (no CYP)

DTG/BIC: CYP3A4 + UGT1A1 substrate

CAB: UGT1A1 substrate | LOW–MODERATE — RAL fewest CYP interactions; DTG/BIC need CYP3A4/UGT1A1 consideration |

NRTIsTenofovir (TDF/TAF), Abacavir (ABC), Lamivudine (3TC), Emtricitabine (FTC), Zidovudine (AZT)Renal transporters (TDF/TAF: OAT1/3, P-gp; ABC: UGT); No CYPLOW — Mainly renal (TDF); ABC hypersensitivity (HLA-B*57:01)

3. 3. ⚡ Critical Interaction Categories

1. 1. TB Drugs (Rifamycins) — CONTRAINDICATED with most ARVs

RifamycinEffect on ARVsManagement
Rifampicin↓ PI/INSTI/NNRTI levels 80–95% (CYP3A4/P-gp induction)CONTRAINDICATED with most PIs, INSTIs (DTG/BIC), RPV/DOR
EFV: ↓ levels ~25% → ↑ EFV dose to 800mg (if weight>50kg)EFV: ↑ to 800mg (with TDM)
DTG: ↓ levels ~50% → ↑ DTG to 50mg BDDTG: 50mg BD with rifampicin
RAL: ↓ levels ~40% → ↑ RAL to 800mg BDRAL: 800mg BD
RifabutinWeaker inducer (~50% effect)Preferred over rifampicin; ↓ PI dose (e.g., ATV/r 300/100mg daily); monitor
DTG: standard doseDTG 50mg OD
Rifampicin + ARV = MAJOR interaction — Avoid if possible; use rifabutin or adjust ARV per guidelines

2. 2. Anticonvulsants (Enzyme Inducers) — MAJOR

AnticonvulsantEffect on ARVsManagement
Carbamazepine, Phenytoin, Phenobarbital↓ PI/INSTI/NNRTI levels (CYP3A4/UGT induction)AVOID if possible; use non-inducing AEDs (Levetiracetam, Lamotrigine, Lacosamide, Valproate)
EFV: ↓ levels ~25%TDM if EFV used
DTG: ↓ levels ~50% → DTG 50mg BDDTG 50mg BD
RAL: ↓ levels ~40% → RAL 800mg BDRAL 800mg BD
RPV/DOR: CONTRAINDICATEDAVOID
Valproate, Levetiracetam, Lamotrigine, Lacosamide, Gabapentin, PregabalinNo significant CYP inductionPreferred AEDs for HIV patients

3. 3. Antifungals (Azoles) — MAJOR (CYP3A4 Inhibition)

AzoleEffect on ARVsManagement
Ketoconazole, Itraconazole, Posaconazole, Voriconazole↑ PI/INSTI levels (CYP3A4 inhibition)Monitor for toxicity; dose adjust PI; avoid with some NNRTIs
DTG/BIC: ↑ levels ~20–30%Usually no dose change; monitor
RAL: No significant interactionSafe
FluconazoleModerate CYP3A4 inhibition; ↑ PI levelsMonitor; ↓ PI dose if needed
DTG/BIC: MinimalUsually no dose change

4. 4. Statins — HIGH RISK (Myopathy/Rhabdo)

StatinPreferred with ARVsAvoid / Dose Limit
AtorvastatinStart LOW (10mg), max 20–40mgAvoid >40mg with boosted PI
RosuvastatinStart LOW (5–10mg), max 20mgAvoid >20mg with boosted PI
PravastatinPreferred (minimal CYP3A4)SAFEST with boosted PI
Simvastatin / Lovastatin—CONTRAINDICATED with boosted PI/Cobi (↑ AUC 10–20x)
Fluvastatin / PitavastatinLow interactionGenerally safe

5. 5. Hormonal Contraception — COMPLEX

ContraceptiveInteractionManagement
Combined OCP (Estrogen + Progestin)EFV/NVP/ETR/Rifampicin: ↓ Ethinylestradiol (↑ failure risk)Add barrier method; consider alternative ARV
Boosted PI: ↑ Progestin (↑ side effects); ↓ EthinylestradiolAdd barrier method
DTG/RAL/BIC: No significant interactionOK
Progestin-only Pill / Implant / InjectionEFV/NVP: ↓ Progestin (↑ failure)Add barrier method; avoid implant with EFV
Boosted PI: ↑ ProgestinUsually OK
IUD (Cu/IUS)No interactionPREFERRED (no enzyme interaction)

6. 6. PPIs / Acid-Reducing Agents — RPV/DOR/ATV/LEDIPASVIR

Acid ReducerAffected ARVManagement
PPIRilpivirine (RPV) — CONTRAINDICATED (↓ absorption)Use H2RA (famotidine) 12h before or 4h after RPV
Atazanavir (ATV) — CONTRAINDICATED (↓ absorption)Avoid PPI; use H2RA 10h before/2h after
Ledipasvir (in Harvoni) — ↓ absorptionSeparate 4h
H2RARPV — Take 12h before or 4h afterOK with timing
ATV — Take 10h before or 2h afterOK with timing
AntacidsGeneral separation 2hStandard

7. 7. Other Key Interactions

DrugARV InteractionManagement
ColchicineBoosted PI: ↑ Colchicine → Toxicity (myopathy, neuropathy)CONTRAINDICATED with boosted PI; avoid
Sildenafil/Tadalafil (PAH)Boosted PI: ↑ Levels → Hypotension, PriapismCONTRAINDICATED for PAH; reduce dose for ED (Sildenafil max 25mg/48h)
Midazolam/Triazolam (Oral)Boosted PI: ↑ Levels → Excessive sedationCONTRAINDICATED (IV midazolam OK with monitoring)
RifampicinSee #1See #1
St John's WortInduces CYP3A4/P-gp → ↓ ARV levelsCONTRAINDICATED

4. 4. 🎯 FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

PearlDetails
Boosted PI (RTV/COBI)Strong CYP3A4 inhibitor — ↑ levels of co-meds (statins, azoles, CCBs, oral midazolam, colchicine, sildenafil PAH)
EFV/NVP/RifampicinCYP3A4 inducers — ↓ levels of PIs, INSTIs, NNRTIs, OCP
Rifampicin + ARVContraindicated with most — use rifabutin or adjust (DTG 50mg BD, RAL 800mg BD, EFV 800mg)
Enzyme-inducing AEDsCarbamazepine/Phenytoin/Phenobarbital — avoid; use Levetiracetam/Lamotrigine
Statins + Boosted PIPravastatin safest; Atorva/Rosuva low dose; Simva/Lova CONTRAINDICATED
OCP + EFV/Boosted PIAdd barrier method — ↓ ethinylestradiol
RPV/DOR + PPICONTRAINDICATED — use H2RA with timing
ATV + PPICONTRAINDICATED

5. 5. ❓ Viva Questions (10)

QAnswer
1. Ritonavir/Cobicistat boosting — effect on CYP3A4?Strong CYP3A4 inhibition → ↑ levels of substrate drugs (statins, CCBs, azoles, midazolam, colchicine, OCP, DOACs)
2. Rifampicin + Dolutegravir — management?Rifampicin induces CYP3A4/UGT1A1 → ↓ DTG ~50% → DTG 50mg BD (instead of 50mg OD)
3. Enzyme-inducing anticonvulsant + DTG — dose?Carbamazepine/Phenytoin/Phenobarbital → DTG 50mg BD (instead of OD)
4. Simvastatin + Darunavir/Ritonavir — safe?NO — CONTRAINDICATED (↑ simvastatin AUC 10–20x → rhabdo); use Pravastatin
5. Rilpivirine + PPI — why contraindicated?RPV requires acidic pH for absorption; PPI ↓ absorption → virological failure
6. EFV + Combined OCP — management?EFV induces CYP3A4 → ↓ ethinylestradiol → Add barrier method; consider alternative ARV
7. Carbamazepine + Atazanavir/Ritonavir — management?Carbamazepine induces CYP3A4 → ↓ ATV/RTV levels → AVOID; switch to non-inducing AED (Levetiracetam)
8. Colchicine + Lopinavir/Ritonavir — safe?NO — CONTRAINDICATED (↑ colchicine → fatal myopathy/neuropathy)
9. Hormonal implant + Efavirenz — failure risk?YES — EFV induces metabolism → ↓ progestin → Add barrier method; avoid implant with EFV
10. St John's Wort + ARVs — effect?Induces CYP3A4/P-gp → ↓ ARV levels → CONTRAINDICATED

6. 6. 🤯 Confusions & Mnemonics

ConfusionClarification
Ritonavir vs CobicistatRTV: CYP3A4 inhibitor + INDUCER (CYP1A2, 2B6, 2C9, 2C19, UGT); COBI: Pure CYP3A4 inhibitor (no induction)
DTG with Rifampicin vs CarbamazepineBoth induce → DTG 50mg BD for both
RAL vs DTG with RifampicinRAL: 800mg BD; DTG: 50mg BD
Pravastatin vs SimvastatinPravastatin = minimal CYP3A4 (safe); Simvastatin = CYP3A4 substrate (CONTRAINDICATED with boosted PI)
RPV + PPI = CONTRAINDICATEDRPV needs acid; ATV also CONTRAINDICATED with PPI
EFV + OCPEFV ↓ ethinylestradiol → barrier method

Mnemonics:

  • "RTV = BOOSTER = CYP3A4 INHIBITOR" = ↑ statins, CCBs, azoles, midazolam, colchicine, OCP, DOACs
  • "RIFAMPICIN = INDUCER = KILLS ARV LEVELS" = Use rifabutin or adjust (DTG BD, RAL BD, EFV 800mg)
  • "EIAED = NO WITH ARV" = Carbamazepine, Phenytoin, Phenobarbital → use Levetiracetam, Lamotrigine
  • "STATINS + BOOSTED PI" = Pravastatin SAFE; Atorva/Rosuva LOW DOSE; Simva/Lova NO
  • "RPV = ACID LOVER" = NO PPI; H2RA with timing
  • "EFV + OCP = BARRIER METHOD"
  • "COLCHICINE + BOOSTED PI = NO"
  • "ST JOHN'S WORT = INDUCER = KILLS ARV"

7. 7. 🧠 Mind Map (Mermaid)

Antiretroviral Interactions
Boosted PI (RTV/COBI)
CYP3A4 STRONG INHIBITOR
↑ Statins, CCBs, Azoles, Midazolam, Colchicine, OCP, DOACs
RTV: also INDUCES CYP1A2/2B6/2C9/2C19/UGT
Inducers (↓ ARV Levels)
Rifampicin (STRONGEST)
– DTG 50mg BD, RAL 800mg BD, EFV 800mg
– Rifabutin preferred (weaker)
Carbamazepine/Phenytoin/Phenobarbital
– AVOID; DTG 50mg BD, RAL 800mg BD
efavirenz/nevirapine (moderate)
Azoles (Inhibitors)
Ketoconazole/Itra/Posa/Vori: ↑ PI/INSTI
Fluconazole: moderate
Statins
Pravastatin: SAFEST
Atorva/Rosuva: Low dose, max 20-40mg
Simva/Lova: CONTRAINDICATED
Contraception
EFV/NVP: ↓ Ethinylestradiol → Barrier
Boosted PI: ↑ Progestin, ↓ Estrogen → Barrier
DTG/RAL/BIC: No interaction
IUD: PREFERRED
Acid Reducers
RPV: CONTRAINDICATED with PPI; H2RA timing
ATV: CONTRAINDICATED with PPI; H2RA timing
Contraindicated
Simva/Lova + Boosted PI
Colchicine + Boosted PI
Sildenafil PAH + Boosted PI
Oral Midazolam/Triazolam + Boosted PI
Rifampicin + most ARVs
St John's Wort

8. 8. 📅 Spaced Repetition Tracker

ReviewDateScoreNext
11d

9. 9. 🧪 Self-Test Scorecard

SectionMaxScore
ARV classes & enzymes8
Rifampicin interactions8
AED interactions8
Statin safety6
Contraception6
Acid reducers6
Contraindicated combos6
Viva answers10
Total56

Target: ≥45/56 (80%)

10. 10. 📝 Exam Answer Modes

1. Short Question (5 marks): "Rifampicin interactions with ARVs and management."

  • Rifampicin = Strong CYP3A4/P-gp/UGT inducer → ↓ PI/INSTI/NNRTI levels 80–95%
  • Contraindicated with most PIs, INSTIs (DTG/BIC), RPV/DOR
  • Management: Use Rifabutin (weaker inducer) OR adjust ARV:
  • DTG: 50mg BD (instead of OD)
  • RAL: 800mg BD (instead of 400mg BD)
  • EFV: 800mg OD (if >50kg, with TDM)
  • Avoid St John's Wort (also induces)

2. Viva (1 min): "Patient on Darunavir/Ritonavir needs simvastatin for hyperlipidaemia. Safe?"

  • NO — Ritonavir strongly inhibits CYP3A4 → ↑ Simvastatin AUC 10–20x → Rhabdomyolysis risk
  • Contraindicated
  • Use Pravastatin (minimal CYP3A4 metabolism) — SAFEST
  • Alternative: Atorvastatin 10–20mg max or Rosuvastatin 5–20mg max with monitoring

3. Ward Round (30 sec): "HIV patient on Efavirenz, started on Carbamazepine for seizures. Viral load rising. Why?"

  • Carbamazepine induces CYP3A4 → ↑ Efavirenz metabolism → ↓ Efavirenz levels → Virological failure
  • AVOID Carbamazepine with EFV
  • Switch to Levetiracetam/Lamotrigine/Lacosamide (non-inducing)
  • Monitor viral load, EFV level if available

4. Last-Night Revision (1-liners):

  • RTV/COBI = CYP3A4 inhibitor → ↑ co-med levels
  • Rifampicin = inducer → DTG BD, RAL BD, EFV 800mg; use Rifabutin
  • EIAEDs (Carba/Phenytoin/Phenobarb) = avoid with ARV; use LEV/LTG/LCM
  • Statins + boosted PI: Pravastatin safest; Simva/Lova NO
  • OCP + EFV/Boosted PI = barrier method
  • RPV/DOR + PPI = CONTRAINDICATED
  • ATV + PPI = CONTRAINDICATED
  • Colchicine + Boosted PI = NO
  • St John's Wort = inducer = NO

11. 11. 📚 Summary Card

ARV INTERACTIONS:
BOOSTED PI (RTV/COBI) = CYP3A4 INHIBITOR → ↑ Statins, CCBs, Azoles, Midazolam, Colchicine, OCP, DOACs
RIFAMPICIN = INDUCER → DTG BD, RAL BD, EFV 800mg; USE RIFABUTIN
EIAEDs (Carba/PHT/PB) = INDUCERS → AVOID; Use LEV/LTG/LCM
STATINS + BOOSTED PI = PRAVASTATIN SAFE; Simva/Lova NO
RPV/ATV + PPI = CONTRAINDICATED
EFV + OCP = BARRIER METHOD
COLCHICINE + BOOSTED PI = NO
ST JOHN'S WORT = INDUCER = NO

12. 12. ❓ MCQs (12)

  1. Ritonavir/Cobicistat boosting — primary mechanism:

A. CYP3A4 induction

B. CYP3A4 inhibition ✓

C. P-gp induction

D. UGT inhibition

E. No CYP effect

  1. Rifampicin + Dolutegravir — correct DTG dose:

A. 50mg OD

B. 50mg BD ✓

C. 50mg TDS

D. 100mg OD

E. Contraindicated

  1. Simvastatin + Darunavir/Ritonavir — management:

A. Safe at standard dose

B. Reduce simvastatin to 10mg

C. Contraindicated; use Pravastatin ✓

D. Monitor CK only

E. Switch to rosuvastatin 40mg

  1. Rilpivirine + PPI — contraindicated because:

A. ↑ RPV levels

B. ↓ RPV absorption (requires acidic pH) ✓

C. ↑ PPI levels

D. QT prolongation

E. Hepatotoxicity

  1. Enzyme-inducing AED + DTG — DTG dose:

A. 50mg OD

B. 50mg BD ✓

C. 50mg TDS

D. 100mg OD

E. Contraindicated

  1. EFV + Combined OCP — management:

A. No action needed

B. Add barrier method ✓

C. Increase OCP dose

D. Stop EFV

E. Switch to progestin-only

  1. Pravastatin + Boosted PI — safe because:

A. CYP3A4 inducer

B. Minimal CYP3A4 metabolism ✓

C. P-gp inhibitor

D. UGT substrate

E. No interaction

  1. Carbamazepine + Atazanavir/Ritonavir — management:

A. Increase ATV dose

B. AVOID; switch to non-inducing AED ✓

C. Monitor ATV level only

D. Reduce RTV dose

E. No interaction

  1. Colchicine + Lopinavir/Ritonavir:

A. Safe

B. Reduce colchicine dose

C. Contraindicated ✓

D. Monitor renal function

E. Use alternative PI

  1. St John's Wort + ARVs:

A. Safe

B. Induces CYP3A4/P-gp → ↓ ARV levels; Contraindicated ✓

C. Inhibits CYP3A4

D. No interaction

E. Only affects NNRTIs

  1. Atazanavir + PPI:

A. Safe

B. Contraindicated ✓

C. Dose adjust PPI

D. Separate by 2h

E. Use H2RA instead

  1. Raltegravir + Rifampicin — RAL dose:

A. 400mg BD

B. 800mg BD ✓

C. 400mg TDS

D. 1200mg BD

E. Contraindicated
